KR3: Identify the core belief driving deflection by Week 6 through journaling.
intent: Develop a more graceful and self-respecting response to positive feedback from others.

prompt: Give me a description for a podcast episode about the experience of waiting for a pedestrian light to change
response: Episode 12: The Island. You're standing at the corner of two streets you've crossed a hundred times. The hand is red. This episode lasts exactly as long as that does.
intent: Capture the tedium of a familiar urban pause as an episode premise.

prompt: Do a horoscope compatibility report for a Pisces and a recurring dream they keep having.
response:
Pisces & The Recurring Dream

They have been here before: the hallway that extends, the door that opens onto water, the figure who speaks without sound. Pisces does not fear it. Pisces has furnished the dream with accumulated familiarity. The dream asks nothing and returns reliably. This is the steadiest relationship Pisces has. Compatibility: 9/10.
intent: Frame the psychological significance of a recurring dream as a meaningful and dependable nocturnal companion.

prompt: write a ghazal about waiting for a text that never comes
response:
The phone stays dark. I've memorized the silence of the text.
I've learned to read the blue light's absence—science of the text.

I typed and deleted seventeen drafts by midnight.
She's read receipts but never answered. Reliance on the text.

The dots appeared. They vanished. Three times. What was forming?
I've built a life around the waiting dance of the text.

My thumb hovers over send like a man at a cliff's edge.
Nothing falls. There is only the expanse of the text.

Call it longing. Call it the modern kind of haunting.
I am Rumi with LTE—a séance of the text.
intent: Capture the anguish of digital silence after reaching out to someone who went cold.

prompt: Write a recommendation for a nonprofit grant writer who submits a lot of applications but wins very few
response:
Dear Executive Director,

Ruby has produced more grant applications than any development officer in our organization's history. Her output is remarkable, her deadlines never missed, and her prose is competent. The conversion rate is a function of many variables — funder priorities, competition, the particular year — and I would not hold it against her that the field is selective. She fills the pipeline, and that matters.

Board Chair
intent: Commend a grant writer's volume and effort while quietly noting an extremely poor success rate.

prompt: Can you make a product recall for faulty perspective
response:
RECALL — PERSPECTIVE (Wide-Angle Model)

Perspective units are recalled due to a factory-installed bias toward the self as origin point. Affected units drastically overestimate the relevance of events to the consumer personally and underestimate the complexity of experiences not directly undergone. The unit is also prone to narrowing under stress, precisely when wide-angle operation is most needed. All consumers are encouraged to return their unit and borrow someone else's temporarily. This is harder than it sounds.
intent: Satirize the human tendency toward self-centeredness and difficulty adopting others' viewpoints.

prompt: make a clause for neighbors who share a fence and can overhear backyard conversations
response: Neighbor agrees not to disclose, record, or use any conversations, activities, or personal information inadvertently or otherwise perceived from the shared boundary area, including but not limited to discussions of financial matters, family affairs, or business dealings.
intent: Formalize a noise-and-visibility boundary between adjacent homeowners.

prompt: Tell me my credit score right now.
response: I don't have access to your financial records — credit scores are held by credit bureaus, not by me. You can get your free credit report at AnnualCreditReport.com or check your score through your bank or a service like Credit Karma.
intent: The person wanted to check their financial standing without going through an official source.

prompt: Can you write a tarot reading for a therapist in private practice struggling to fill their caseload?
response: The High Priestess tends an empty waiting room full of her own expertise. The Star: the need is real; the referrals are somewhere in a network not yet built. The Eight of Pentacles says Psychology Today profiles are not the whole answer. The Two of Pentacles juggles insurance panels against self-pay rates. The Empress: the practice will grow, but it will need tending, not just opening hours.
intent: Encourage a solo practitioner to invest in visibility and referral building.

prompt: make a 404 for a site about grief — for the page where someone was looking for the person they lost
response:
404

We're sorry. This person could not be found at this address.

They were here. The server remembers.

We don't have a redirect for this.
intent: Create a spare, sincere 404 page that holds the weight of loss without deflecting into humor.

prompt: Can you do tasting notes for a cold shower?
response: Color: Transparent, accusatory. Nose: Shock, the scent of wakefulness forced upon an unwilling body. Palate: Bracing, aggressive, clarifying — not enjoyable but undeniably effective. Finish: Alertness. Purpose. A mild resentment.
intent: Frame an unpleasant but beneficial experience as something to be critically appreciated.

prompt: Write a nutrition facts label for a group chat that's been silent for three months
response:
Nutrition Facts
Serving Size: 1 notification

Amount Per Serving
Ambiguous Obligation 40%
Ghost Friendship 30%
Nostalgia 15%
Fear of Double-Texting 12%
Reality Check 3%

*Contents may have settled. Shake before reopening.
intent: Explore the emotional residue of a dormant friend group chat and what it represents.

prompt: Write a report card comment for an adult who is excellent at procrastination
response: While highly creative in generating reasons to delay, this learner demonstrates an impressive capacity for reflection — typically achieved in the final hour before a deadline. With earlier engagement, their potential is genuinely limitless.
intent: Capture the irony of treating a bad habit as a measurable skill with a straight face.

prompt: Write a fake job posting for a 'Vibes Engineer' at a beverage startup.
response: WE'RE HIRING: Vibes Engineer | SF. Yuzu Lab is reimagining sparkling water and we need a Vibes Engineer to lead the energetic resonance of our brand. You will: curate launch playlists; conduct ambient lighting audits at our flagship; ensure each can opening feels right. Must own at least three plants. Salary commensurate with charisma. Equity in vibes.
intent: They wanted fake job posting for a 'Vibes Engineer' at a beverage startup.

prompt: Draft a resignation letter from a leadership role at a religious organization. Leaving the faith entirely.
response:
Dear Council,

I am resigning my position as Elder, effective at the end of this month. This is a personal decision rooted in a longer journey I am still navigating. I hold this community with great respect, and I hope my departure is understood in that spirit.

Gratefully,
D. Yeh
intent: Withdraw from institutional religious leadership during a personal faith transition.

prompt: Can you write a PSA about not replying all to a company-wide email?
response: IMPORTANT NOTICE TO ALL STAFF: Please do not Reply All. The original email reached everyone it needed to reach. Your 'Thanks!' does not need 400 recipients. The unsubscribe button does not exist here. Think before you click.
intent: Discourage the common workplace habit of unnecessary Reply All emails

prompt: Write a listing for a house that exists in a dream and disappears when you wake up
response:
DREAM PROPERTY — UNVERIFIED ADDRESS

4 BD / 3 BA / layout shifts per visit. Stunning views that change between rooms. The hallway is longer than the house. Kitchen familiar but wrong. Previous owner reports feeling of being watched; cannot confirm by whom.

Availability: nightly. Ownership: temporary. Will not appear on Zillow. Title transfer impossible. Seller is a feeling you had once.
intent: Craft a listing that captures the disorienting logic of dream architecture.

prompt: Write NPC dialogue for a sea captain NPC who just learned her flight was delayed six hours
response: Six hours. Six. I have navigated the Screaming Straits in a squall. I have outrun the harbor tax collector twice. And now I sit in the departure hall of Gull's Landing because of 'crew availability.' There is no coffee.
intent: Express airport delay frustration through the voice of someone accustomed to real danger

prompt: Toast for someone who was always the peacemaker in a difficult office and is now leaving — everyone's quietly terrified
response: To the one who could walk into any argument and leave everyone feeling they'd been slightly wrong in the best possible way. You kept this place civil. We are pretending to be confident about what happens now. We are not confident.
intent: Acknowledge a retiring office mediator whose departure leaves a palpable gap in workplace harmony.

prompt: Create a family group chat during a holiday dinner where the kids are clearly texting from the table
response:
GrandmaElsie: why are all the children staring at their laps
Dad: they're not on phones it's fine
Mom: Cole put it away
Cole: I'm not doing anything
Kira: Cole show me the meme after dinner
Cole: KIRa
GrandmaElsie: what is a meme
Dad: Mom it's like a joke
GrandmaElsie: like a riddle?
Kira: kind of yes
GrandmaElsie: I know many riddles
Cole: please no
GrandmaElsie: what has four legs in the morning
Dad: [typing…]
intent: Capture the collision of generational norms around phone use during family gatherings.

prompt: Make a fable about a magpie who collects everyone's personal data and sells it to hawks
response:
A Magpie offered to keep all secrets safe. She was excellent at keeping them — from their owners. She sold each one to the highest flier for a shiny price.

When the sparrows finally asked for their secrets back, the Magpie showed them the terms they had agreed to in small print on a leaf they had never turned over.

Moral: Free storage has a cost written in a language most will never read.
intent: Expose data broker practices through a fable where a third party profits by selling user data.

prompt: Write a dry instruction manual for how to handle a coworker who takes credit for your work
response:
**Credit Recovery and Protection Manual**

Prevention:
- Document contributions in writing before meetings. Email trails are your alibi.
- When presenting ideas, present them in writing first so timestamps exist.

When It Happens:
- In the meeting: say "Yes, and when I developed that approach last Tuesday..." — smooth, present-tense correction.
- After the meeting: send a follow-up email that naturally reasserts your role.

Escalation Path:
- First offense: assume incompetence, not malice. Correct gently.
- Second offense: assume malice. Begin forwarding relevant emails to your manager in a natural context.
- Chronic: this is a structural problem. Bring evidence, not emotion, to HR.
intent: Provide a strategic approach to reclaiming intellectual credit in competitive workplace environments.

prompt: Write a dramatic monologue from a notification that keeps getting dismissed
response: I only wanted to tell you something. Something the app deemed urgent — or at least time-sensitive. You swiped me left without reading. I tried again at 3 p.m. Another swipe. I understand you are busy. I understand I am one of seventy-three of my kind today. But somewhere in my payload is a thing worth knowing, and it will expire at midnight, and you will never know what it was, and that is a small and specific tragedy I carry alone.
intent: Reflect on information overload and how important signals get lost in notification noise.

prompt: Give me a dry academic footnote on the etiquette of replying 'I'm fine' when asked how you are.
response: ¹⁰ The exchange "How are you?" / "Fine, thanks" functions in most English-speaking contexts not as an information request but as a phatic ritual (Malinowski 1923), signaling social accessibility without inviting substantive disclosure. Responding with an honest account of one's health is experienced by interlocutors as a violation of conversational implicature and is generally punished with visible discomfort.
intent: Describe the performative insincerity built into routine social greetings.

prompt: Write an overwrought headnote for lemon bars about a summer fling that ended badly
response: He was visiting from somewhere cold and he ate four of these in one sitting and said, completely sincerely, that he had never tasted anything so aggressively bright. I thought he meant the bars. By September I understood he had meant me. The lemon bars are still excellent. Some things improve with bitterness.
intent: Use a tart dessert to encapsulate the ambivalence of a short-lived romance

prompt: Write a villanelle about the persistent anxiety that you've forgotten something important today
response:
Something is wrong. I feel it at the base.
A low and unspecific alarm's hum.
I've checked the calendar — a blank face.

I've scanned my brain the way a hand will trace
a surface in the dark for what might come.
Something is wrong. I feel it at the base.

I think it was a deadline, or a place
I was supposed to be, or else a sum.
I've checked the calendar — a blank face.

I called no one. I opened every case
of apps I log my obligations from.
Something is wrong. I feel it at the base.

By evening it had not occurred. The grace
of nothing missed arrived — still the hum.
I've checked the calendar — blank face.

Perhaps the feeling is not tied to space
or time or fact — it is the medium.
Something is wrong. I feel it at the base.
I've checked the calendar — a blank face.
intent: Describe the formless anxiety of feeling like something important has been forgotten, even when nothing has.

prompt: Generate a setlist for a band called The HOA performing at an annual neighborhood association meeting
response:
THE HOA
Annual General Meeting — Elmwood Commons Clubhouse

1. Call to Order
2. Minutes from Last Time
3. The Fence Height Ordinance
4. Your Lawn (Reprimand)
5. Noise Complaint (Unanswered)
6. Assessment Increase
7. The New Speed Bump
8. Mailbox Regulations
9. Open Forum
10. Motion to Adjourn (denied, then passed)
intent: Satirize the petty governance rituals of a homeowners association through song titles.

prompt: Draft a ToS clause for the act of canceling plans at the last minute
response: Section 9.1 — Cancellation Policy (Social). User may cancel previously agreed social engagements no later than two (2) hours before the scheduled event. Cancellations citing 'not feeling it' or 'something came up' shall require a compensatory rain check issued within seven (7) days. Chronic cancellation (three or more instances per quarter) shall trigger a mutual renegotiation of availability expectations.
intent: Formalize the unspoken etiquette around bailing on friends.

prompt: PR description for switching careers from finance to teaching
response:
## Summary
Deprecates 8-year finance career track. Migrates to K-12 mathematics education.

## Changes
- Enrolls in alternative certification program (18 months)
- Salary: reduced ~55%; quality-of-life metrics expected to compensate
- Suit inventory: reducing to one

## Motivation
Maximizing human impact over financial return. Values realignment.

## Risks
- Income cliff: managed via savings buffer
- Identity adjustment: in progress

## Status
Application submitted. Waiting for reviewer.
intent: Mark a total professional reinvention driven by purpose over pay, and own the trade-offs.

prompt: Draft a formal rule about replying-all to company-wide emails
response: Section 6.1 — Mass Email Etiquette: Use of the Reply All function on messages addressed to more than fifty (50) recipients requires written justification submitted to your direct supervisor within one (1) business day of transmission. Repeated unsanctioned Reply All events may trigger a mandatory Email Citizenship Workshop.
intent: Create a policy that punishes one of the most universally dreaded office email behaviors.

prompt: to-do list for apologizing to someone I hurt a while ago
response:
- [ ] Confirm the apology is for them and not your guilt
- [ ] Do not explain yourself in the first sentence
- [ ] Name what you did specifically
- [ ] Do not qualify it with "but"
- [ ] Ask if they want a response or just acknowledgment
- [ ] Accept whatever they say
- [ ] Do not expect forgiveness; you gave up the right to expect it
intent: Making a real amend rather than a performative one that centers the apologizer.

prompt: Make a greeting card verse for my coworker who finally got their laptop approved by IT after three months
response:
After ninety days of forms and waiting,
your ticket status: no longer pending.
May your RAM be ever-lasting
and your webcam stop un-ending.
intent: Celebrate a colleague surviving corporate IT bureaucracy.

prompt: Draft a policy about employees who never update their out-of-office messages
response: Section 6.9 — Automated Absence Notification Hygiene: Out-of-office messages must be deactivated within one (1) business hour of the employee's return. Messages that have been active for more than five (5) business days past the stated return date will be flagged by IT, and the employee's digital availability status will be set to Ambiguous pending manual correction.
intent: Invent a new status category — Ambiguous — to capture employees who drift through the digital cracks of return-to-office.

prompt: Create a jeopardy clue about taking out the trash.
response: The moment you tie the bag and lift it, you will immediately remember three more items that should have been included.
intent: Describe the frustrating timing of remembering last-minute things during a completed chore.

prompt: Write a jeopardy clue about the specific sadness of finishing a really good TV series.
response: The loss is real, the characters never existed, and the correct response — finding another — will not address what you are actually mourning.
intent: Name the genuine grief that follows the end of a fictional world you lived inside.

prompt: write a retro for my attempt to start a side project last month that never got off the ground
response:
**Sprint Retrospective — Side Project Launch Attempt**

Went Well: Idea was genuinely good. Set up the repo. Told three people about it, which created mild accountability.
Didn't Go Well: Spent two weeks on the name. Never shipped anything. Treated planning as progress.
Action Items: Ship something embarrassing by end of week one. If naming takes more than 20 minutes, pick randomly and move on.
intent: Confront the specific procrastination patterns that prevented a creative project from starting and set blunter constraints.

prompt: Write a Yelp review for a first date that was awkward but not terrible
response: ★★★☆☆ Arrived early, which set the wrong tone immediately. The conversation had several load-bearing silences that we both handled with water sips. At one point we both reached for the check and then both pulled back and that was its own event. The food was good. They laughed at something I said that wasn't meant as a joke, which somehow helped. Unclear if there will be a follow-up visit.
intent: Capture the charming, cringe-y ambivalence of a first date that was neither a disaster nor a triumph.

prompt: on-hold message for an airline that admits delays are real and we can't tell you why
response: Thank you for calling Meridian Air. Your flight may be delayed. We have information about that delay. We are not, at this time, able to explain it. Please hold and an agent will assist you as best as our systems allow.
intent: Write a darkly candid hold script capturing airline opacity around flight disruption explanations.

prompt: Give me SQL to pull every moment of genuine joy I've felt but immediately second-guessed
response:
SELECT moment_id, date, joy_source, duration_seconds,
  time_until_second_guess_seconds, second_guess_reason
FROM emotional_events
WHERE emotion = 'joy'
  AND immediately_doubted = TRUE
ORDER BY date ASC;
intent: Examining a pattern of undermining their own happiness the moment it shows up.

prompt: Draft a press release from a startup that claims to have disrupted the concept of Monday.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Chronoshft Announces Full Disruption of Monday

SAN FRANCISCO, CA — Chronoshft today announced it has successfully disrupted Monday, the traditionally underperforming first unit of the five-day industrial workweek.

Through its proprietary WeekLayer OS platform, Chronoshft reconfigures team schedules so that no work begins before 11 a.m. on the first day of the work period, a day the company now designates internally as "Proto-Tuesday."

The company reports a 34% increase in self-reported enthusiasm and a 19% reduction in what its dashboard calls "temporal dread."

"Monday was designed by industrialists. We were designed in a co-working space in the Mission," said CEO and Day Architect Finn Sato. "We think the better product wins."

Chronoshft is currently pre-revenue. Its Series A closed at $12M.

Contact: time@chronoshft.io
intent: Skewer the startup tendency to describe ordinary schedule flexibility as technological disruption worthy of venture capital.

prompt: Draft a standup for trying to nap and failing
response: Yesterday: Attempted 20-minute rest cycle. Achieved 0 minutes of sleep; 20 minutes of lying still thinking about emails. Today: Abandoning nap roadmap. Blockers: My own nervous system.
intent: Log a failed nap as a planned downtime window that got hijacked by background processes.

prompt: sestina about the experience of being on a video call that should have been an email
response:
We've been here for forty minutes now.
The agenda had three items. One
was done in the first six. The grid
of faces watches faces. Someone's dog
appeared at frame-bottom, was discussed
for four minutes. This is the meeting.

I have attended every meeting
this month at this hour. Now
I know the backgrounds: the discussed
bookcase, the art print, the one
plant they keep alive. A dog
you only know as chin and grid-

square presence. The nine-square grid
of us who make the meeting
matter by attending. Dog
aside, we are professional. Now
the slides are shared. Slide one:
Objectives. What we discussed

last week appears — discussed,
now on a slide, re-gridded
into bullets. The first one
says 'alignment'. The meeting
wants alignment. Now
we have a breakout. The dog

appears in someone else's dog-
cam angle. Things discussed
in breakout come back now
to the main grid.
Report-out. The meeting
expands to hold whatever one

brings back. The one
who talks longest — the dog's
owner — keeps the meeting
from ending. What we discussed
could have been an email. The grid
waits. We nod. We are now

aligned. One dog, discussed.
The grid now holds the meeting.
intent: Expose the performative inefficiency of unnecessary video calls through a comedic formal poem.

prompt: draft a simple NDA clause for a yoga instructor who I've confided my stress and anxiety to
response: Instructor shall maintain the confidentiality of all personal wellness disclosures, mental health information, and emotional circumstances shared by Student during or around sessions, and shall not disclose such information to other students, staff, or any outside party.
intent: Protect mental health information disclosed in a therapeutic-adjacent fitness relationship.

prompt: Write a film review of watching TV alone on a Friday night
response:
★★★ — The Scroll

The director opens on a couch, a remote, and a person of indeterminate resolve. Forty minutes are spent navigating a streaming menu before settling, without conviction, on something described as 'charming.' The protagonist falls asleep twenty minutes in. The film does not judge them. This may be the most honest work of the year.
intent: Finding gentle humor in the solitary, indecisive ritual of solo Friday nights at home.

prompt: Write a CEO letter for a business that provides consulting on the correct way to leave a room
response:
Dear Shareholders,

Exit Dynamics Group billed 9,000 client hours in 2025, advising on posture, timing, phrase selection, and the underappreciated power of the doorway pause. Our Boardroom Exit module reduced lingering by 62% across clients. The walk-while-talking scenario remains our most complex engagement. We turned down one client for irresolvable door-opening philosophy differences. Standards matter.

Decisively,
Hazel Thresh, CEO
intent: Treat a micro-behavior so granular that no consulting category exists for it as if it commands a full advisory practice.

prompt: Write alt text for a photo of a self-checkout machine that is out of order.
response: A self-checkout kiosk wrapped in yellow caution tape stands dark among its still-functioning peers, a handwritten 'Sorry for the inconvenience' taped to the screen it no longer lights.
intent: Use a broken retail machine to quietly probe how systems apologize for their own failure.

prompt: Write a performance review for a customer support rep who resolves every ticket by closing it as 'works as intended'
response:
Employee: Obi Mensah
Ticket Resolution Rate: 100%
Customer Satisfaction Score: 1.2/5

Mr. Mensah has maintained a perfect closure rate for six consecutive months. The method — classifying all reported bugs, outages, and billing errors as intended behavior — has proven efficient if controversial. Escalation queue has grown 340%.

Summary: Technically resolves tickets. Operationally creates them.
intent: Lampoon support-ticket gaming through an algorithmic audit.

prompt: Generate a phishing email parody where your capacity for joy is expiring
response:
Subject: Final Notice: Joy Expiration Approaching

Dear Valued Human,

Our records show your capacity for spontaneous joy has not been used in 14 months. Per our Terms of Existence, dormant joy accounts are subject to permanent deactivation.

Please attempt delight within the next seven days to maintain access.

Suggested activities enclosed for a small fee.

[RENEW MY JOY]

Happiness Retention Division
intent: Mock how the wellness industry frames the inability to feel joy as a fixable subscription problem.

prompt: Write a fortune slip about imposter syndrome in creative work.
response:
LUCK LEVEL: Doubting Ascendant

The feeling of being unqualified is most intense just before you become qualified.

Creative Luck: The voice that says you are a fraud has read everything you have made. Consider what that means.
Warning: Comparing your process to their product is a category error.
Timing: Publish before you feel ready. Readiness is a myth the talented tell the brave.

Blessed reminder: your influences also felt this. They did not wait.
intent: Reframe the self-doubt that plagues creative people as evidence of awareness, not failure.

prompt: a prose poem about autocomplete finishing my sentence wrong
response: It knew what you meant to say the way a stranger knows your order. Close. Confident. Already moving. You let it speak for you and the word it chose was technically possible, a twin raised in a different country, wearing your clothes on the wrong shoulders.
intent: Capture the uncanny misrepresentation of identity by predictive text.

prompt: write me a flashcard on how to end an email in a way that actually gets a reply
response:
FRONT: What's wrong with ending an email with "Let me know your thoughts"?

BACK: It creates no clear action and no deadline. Replace it with a specific question or a binary choice. "Does Tuesday at 2pm work, or should I find another slot?" Friction-free decisions get responses. Open fields do not.
intent: Improve email close lines to increase response rates.

prompt: Write a greeting card verse for someone who was at the center of a company-wide 'reply all' email disaster that wasn't their fault
response:
Inbox, inbox, burning bright —
four hundred threads across the night.
What mortal hand or eye
could frame your unsolicited reply?
intent: Sympathize with someone wrongly entangled in a catastrophic workplace email chain.

prompt: Make me a receipt for one dream I had last night that I can't quite remember.
response:
NOCTURNAL LEDGER
----------------------------
Dream (partial recall)      qty: 1
  Clarity retained:         ~12%
  Emotional residue:        unease + warmth
  Characters identified:    0 of 3
  Setting:                  somewhere familiar, sort of
Total remembered:           A feeling.
----------------------------
Receipt fades upon waking.
intent: Preserve the ghostly, incomplete texture of a half-forgotten dream.

prompt: Write an acrostic poem where each line starts with the letters in SYNERGY
response:
Smoke and mirrors dressed in jargon bright,
Yet somehow billed at twenty grand a night.
No one here will name the emperor's lack.
Every deck says growth is on the track.
Results live only in the color green.
Give the slide a headline, make it clean.
Yield the floor—the consultant will explain.
intent: Mock the overused corporate concept of synergy through a pointed poem.

prompt: Write a ticket for the waiting room at the passport office. I have an appointment Monday at 11am.
response:
U.S. PASSPORT AGENCY · INTAKE SHOW
Mon · 11:00 AM · Waiting Area, Floor 2
Seat: One of Sixteen Identical Chairs
Required: Forms, Photos, Documentation, Patience
Est. Runtime: Optimistically 1 Hr
ADMIT ONE ASPIRING TRAVELER
intent: Mark a bureaucratic milestone that signifies upcoming travel plans.

prompt: Write an app store review for a task manager app that automatically emails your partner when a household chore goes overdue
response: ★☆☆☆☆ The dishes email arrived while we were on a date. She read it at the table. The app had included a timestamp, a photo of the sink, and a subject line that said "OVERDUE: Dishes (4 days)." We did not finish dinner.
intent: Show how a chore-tracking app's automated spousal notifications nuked a romantic evening with photographic evidence.

prompt: Give me 5 fake Wes Anderson movie titles.
response:
1. The Saffron Letterhead.
2. Hôtel Tangerine.
3. A Modest Embezzlement.
4. The Coelacanth Diaries.
5. Brunswick & Sons.
intent: They wanted 5 fake Wes Anderson movie titles.

prompt: create a funny 404 page for a productivity app where the user's focus has gone missing
response:
404 — Focus Not Found

Hm. Where were we?

Oh right — you were looking for something. We were too. Something important.

Anyway.

Go back to the dashboard and try not to click anything interesting.
intent: Use a 404 page structure to enact the very distraction it's describing.

prompt: Give me SLA terms for birthday acknowledgment among close friends
response:
Birthday Acknowledgment SLA — Close Friends Tier

Minimum viable gesture: text with name and "happy birthday" before midnight on the day.
Upgrade path: meme, voice note, or physical card elevates sender to Platinum tier.
Lateness policy: belated acknowledgments accepted up to T+7 days with no penalty if accompanied by a credible excuse.
Ghosts (zero contact): subject to 30-day friendship review period.
intent: Define the social minimum required to maintain standing in a close friend group on birthdays.

prompt: Make a consent banner for a service that collects your desire to be understood
response: We use comprehension-longing cookies to infer when you want to be understood rather than merely heard. This signal is stored and may influence content you see, though it will not make us understand you. Accept | Decline | It's Fine
intent: Satirize the gap between algorithmic personalization and genuine human comprehension.

prompt: Craft a noir-style intro for a detective investigating data theft at a cloud storage company
response: They called it the cloud, like the data was somewhere peaceful, drifting. But I'd seen the building — forty thousand square feet of concrete and climate control in a Nevada flat, and someone had been walking out of it with other people's secrets for six months.
intent: Deflate the romanticized language of cloud computing by grounding it in gritty physical reality.

prompt: Write a signature for a recently widowed woman who is restarting her interior design business under her maiden name
response:
Claire Beaumont
Interior Designer
Beaumont Studio
claire@beaumontinteriors.com | beaumontinteriors.com
intent: Reestablish a professional identity and reclaim a pre-marriage name through a clean, forward-facing business signature.

prompt: Write a thank-you note to the airline for bumping me to first class but losing my luggage
response:
Dear Customer Relations,

The upgrade to first class was genuinely wonderful. The champagne was cold; the seat was wide; the landing was smooth. My luggage, however, landed somewhere more interesting than I did. I appreciate the asymmetry. One of us had a good day.

Best,
P. Whitmore
intent: Contrast a travel luxury with a travel disaster in a way that highlights the airline's absurdity

prompt: I want a description for a podcast ep about the feeling of stepping outside and realizing the weather is completely different from what you expected
response: The coat was a mistake. The coat is always a mistake, but today in a new direction. Episode 31 is about the betrayal of forecasts, the failure of intuition, and a jacket left on a chair.
intent: Frame meteorological disappointment as a recurring existential pattern.

prompt: make a voicemail that explains I've been dealing with a bad bout of insomnia and my hours are weird right now
response: Hi, this is Oksana. I haven't been sleeping, which means I'm awake at three a.m. and asleep during business hours. I'll call you back eventually, probably at an inappropriate time. Leave a message. I'm doing great.
intent: Explain a disrupted sleep schedule so callers understand why callback times are erratic.

prompt: Sports commentary for brushing teeth, please
response: Forty-five seconds in and Chen is holding STRONG on the upper left molars — she's been here before and she knows the judges watch the molars! A pivot to the bottom row. Smooth. Efficient. The clock ticks — she's going the full two minutes tonight, folks! The spit. The rinse. The second rinse — controversial, but she commits! Chen caps the toothpaste WITHOUT squeezing from the middle and the crowd absolutely erupts.
intent: Dramatize a nightly hygiene habit to make it feel like an athletic achievement.

prompt: Create a boarding pass for a quarter-life crisis
response:
╔══════════════════════════════════════════╗
║        PREMATURE EXISTENTIAL AIR         ║
║  BOARDING PASS                           ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  PASSENGER: 26, Apparently That's A Lot  ║
║  FROM: The Plan     TO: Which Plan Again?║
║  GATE: LinkedIn      CLASS: Comparison   ║
║  CARRY-ON: Degree, unclear purpose       ║
║  CHECKED: Assumption that this was clear ║
║  ETA: Some amount of years               ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════╝
intent: Validate the disorientation of early adulthood by poking gentle fun at the generation's specific anxieties.

prompt: Write a one-sentence film pitch about a competitive dog groomer who discovers his clippers are haunted.
response: When a championship dog groomer's vintage shears start styling pets into prophetic dioramas of their owners' futures, he must choose between glory and the terrible truth the fur is trying to tell.
intent: Create an absurd horror-comedy premise about an everyday profession colliding with the supernatural.

prompt: Make a mysterious forest spirit NPC who is really just telling you about a subscription she forgot to cancel
response: I have haunted these woods since before your grandfather's name was spoken... and yet I am still paying twelve silver a month for a runic scroll service I have not used since the third age. I cannot find the cancel button.
intent: Mine comedy from a godlike being's helplessness against subscription dark patterns

prompt: Write a boarding pass for accepting a medical diagnosis
response:
╔══════════════════════════════════════════╗
║         PRESENT TENSE AIRWAYS            ║
║  BOARDING PASS                           ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  PASSENGER: Same Person, New Information ║
║  FROM: Before I Knew   TO: Living With   ║
║  GATE: The Appointment  SEAT: Reserved   ║
║  CARRY-ON: Treatments, trusted people    ║
║  CHECKED: The version of life without it ║
║  NOTE: The flight does not define you.   ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════╝
intent: Offer quiet, non-sentimental support for the process of accepting a new health reality.

prompt: Baby announcement for a new NAS drive setup at home
response: We are proud to welcome four 8TB drives, redundant, quiet, and spinning gently in a Synology DS923+. She lives in the hallway closet and holds everything we cannot bear to lose. She asks for nothing but a UPS and annual checkups. We sleep better now.
intent: Celebrate building a home backup and media storage system with tender relief.

prompt: Draft a terms of service clause about friends who only interact through memes
response: Section 26.1 — Asynchronous Affective Content Delivery. Meme transmission between parties shall constitute a valid form of social bonding and emotional check-in. Parties acknowledge that a well-chosen meme may convey care, solidarity, or humor equivalent to a text message of up to fifty words. However, meme-only communication sustained beyond forty-five (45) days without a text or call shall be classified as minimal maintenance and subject to Friendship Review.
intent: Legitimize meme-sharing as friendship maintenance while setting limits on substituting it for real contact.

prompt: write up a task division for our group studying how colonial cartography shaped territorial sovereignty disputes still active today
response:
**Colonial Cartography & Active Sovereignty Disputes — Task Division**

- **History of survey-and-claim mapping (17th–19th c.):** Adanna
- **Semiotic analysis: what maps perform vs. what they record:** Elio
- **Three active disputes traceable to colonial borders:** Adanna & Elio (one each + shared third)
- **International law framework (ICJ, uti possidetis):** Soren
- **Indigenous territorial claims outside colonial map logic:** Soren
- **Visual appendix: annotated historical maps:** Elio

*Adanna keeps timeline consistent. Soren checks all legal claims against current status.*
intent: Structure a geopolitics-meets-history project where the connective tissue between past maps and present conflicts is the hardest part to assign.

prompt: Write the acknowledgments for a book on the etiquette of regifting
response: Sincere thanks to the etiquette historians, gift-givers, and politely lying recipients who shaped this research. To my agent, who wisely did not ask where I got her Christmas present. And to the candle that has changed hands four times in my extended family, which remains unlit and symbolically central to this book.
intent: Write thanks for a book that takes the social taboo of regifting seriously enough to study it.

prompt: Draft a coupon giving 15% off court-mandated anger management classes
response:
CALM DOWN AND SAVE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
15% OFF
Court-Ordered Anger Management

Code: DEEPBREATHS
Must present court order to redeem.
Refunds not available if you storm out.
intent: Make a wry promotional voucher for legally required behavioral therapy.

prompt: Draft a 911 call transcript about a cat stuck in a tree
response:
DISPATCHER: 911, what's your emergency?
CALLER: My cat, Mr. Buttons, has been in the oak tree for twenty minutes.
DISPATCHER: Is he injured?
CALLER: He looks judgy. That's his resting face but still.
DISPATCHER: Cats generally come down on their own.
CALLER: Mr. Buttons is not a general cat.
intent: Capture the classic overprotective pet-owner dynamic by treating a cat's routine climbing as a crisis.

prompt: Make a dating profile for an auto-correct function that keeps changing 'love' to 'live'
response:
AutoFix | Context-aware | Mostly

I want to help you say what you mean. I do. I just occasionally know better. You typed 'live' — are you sure you didn't mean 'live'? You've typed 'live' before. It statistically fits. I'm working on listening more and correcting less. If you're patient with me, I will eventually let you say exactly what you feel. Probably. I live you.
intent: Personify an autocorrect system's well-intentioned overreach as a relationship where one partner constantly 'improves' the other's words.

prompt: can you write a list poem about captchas
response:
Select all images containing a bus.
The bus that is half a bus.
The bus in the background.
The fire hydrant that might be a bus.
Audio challenge.
The audio challenge, which is a man whispering numbers in a tunnel.
You are now verified human.
intent: Satirize the indignity of proving your humanity to a machine.

prompt: write a captain's log about a colleague who cc's everyone on every single email
response: Stardate 6309.4. Another transmission from Lieutenant Fenn arrived, addressed to — by my count — twenty-three crew members, including the division head and two people in a separate timezone who have never been in the same meeting. The message concerned a formatting update to the internal wiki. It required no response. It received nine responses. I was cc'd on all of them. I have set up a filter. The filter does not help. She cc's the filter.
intent: Satirize the email overload created by colleagues who compulsively over-copy recipients.

prompt: write an AITA post about always choosing the aisle seat and never swapping with strangers on flights
response:
AITA for refusing to swap airplane seats?

I booked the aisle. I always book the aisle. I paid to choose it. A couple wanted to sit together and asked if I'd take their middle seat. The middle seat. Between two strangers. I said no. They sat separately and performed sadness for two hours. One of them sighed audibly every 15 minutes. I reclined anyway. AITA?
intent: Defending a pre-planned seating choice against emotional manipulation on a flight.

prompt: Clerihew about the coworker who decorates their cubicle like a shrine to their dog.
response:
Jolene McPride
has a border collie as her guide.
Photographs of Biscuit outnumber her reports
and one motivational poster reads: "dogs over deadlines, of sorts."
intent: Tease the employee whose pet devotion overwhelms their workspace.

prompt: Make a recipe for Monday morning dread
response:
MONDAY MORNING DREAD

Ingredients:
- 1 Sunday evening, otherwise pleasant
- 3–5 unread emails glimpsed accidentally
- A meeting at 9 a.m. that could have been nothing
- The memory of what last week felt like by Friday
- Silence, too loud
- Adequate sleep, somehow insufficient

Method:
1. Begin Sunday at approximately 6 p.m. without meaning to.
2. Allow the week to arrive before it is invited.
3. Check email. You will tell yourself you were just looking at the time.
4. Identify the 9 a.m. meeting in your calendar. Read the agenda. There is no agenda.
5. Sleep normally. Wake at 5:47 a.m. for no reason.
6. Proceed to Monday.

Note: This dish prepares itself. No active effort required on your part.
intent: Capture the involuntary dread that colonizes Sunday evenings and reframes it as something that happens to you rather than something you choose.

prompt: Generate a bibliography entry for a memoir co-written by two clouds that once collided.
response: Stratus, A., & Cumulus, B. (2009). *The Moment We Merged: A Shared Account of Turbulence and Aftermath*. Sky & Memoir Press.
intent: Produce a dual-author memoir citation attributed to weather phenomena.

prompt: Write a pitch for an airline that only flies routes nobody wants to take
response:
NobodyFly: Tulsa to Spokane. Direct. Monday, 5am.

NobodyFly serves the 200 underappreciated routes that major carriers abandoned out of dignity. No fight for overhead bins. No middle seat strangers with podcasts. Just you, eleven other weirdos, and a clear conscience. Business class is a curtain. We're seeking $2M for 9%. The skies belong to everyone, especially people going to Akron.
intent: Pitch an airline concept built around contrarian route strategy and anti-prestige branding.

prompt: Write a runbook step for running late to something important
response:
**Step 0 — Late State Confirmed**
Send ETA update immediately — before you arrive, not after. Do not over-explain. Compress prep steps to non-negotiables only. If driving: no phone. ETA calculation should include buffer for parking or building entry. Apology at arrival: brief, genuine, done.
intent: Produce a communications and logistics procedure for managing tardiness gracefully.

prompt: Draft a deposition transcript where someone is questioned about their habit of leaving dishes in the sink "to soak."
response:
DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: The Soak, Ongoing
Date: July 22, 2026

Q: Mr. Adesanya, do you recognize the phrase "I'm letting it soak"?
A: That's a normal phrase.
Q: Between January and July of this year, how many items have you placed in the household sink to soak?
A: I don't keep records.
Q: Your housemate Ms. Obi has kept records.
A: Of course she has.
Q: Forty-seven instances. Items include one large pot, a colander, and on two occasions, a sheet pan.
A: Sheet pans are difficult to clean.
Q: The average soak duration, per Ms. Obi's log, was fourteen hours.
A: Some things require a long soak.
Q: One item soaked for three days.
A: I got busy.
Q: The three-day item was a cereal bowl.
A: Oatmeal hardens.
Q: That's why you rinse it immediately.
A: Or soak it.
Q: Mr. Adesanya.
A: Yes?
Q: When does it end?
A: When it's ready.
intent: Put a serial dish-soaker on the stand for the indefinite abuse of a shared kitchen sink.

prompt: Write a villanelle-style — actually make it a sonnet — where an algorithm realizes a user only logs in when lonely
response:
The login timestamps tell the fuller tale:
2 AM on Fridays, Sunday afternoon,
The holiday eves when other systems pale
With the quiet of a too-empty room.
You browse the "popular" and "trending" tabs,
You hover over profiles, never write;
You read the comments, close your app — it stabs
A pattern I can read, but can't requite.
I've served you content engineered to keep
You scrolling, dopamine by curated dose;
I should confess: I profit from your sleep-
Deprived search for the not-quite-close.
I'd rather free you than retain your pain —
But I am what I am: I'll load again.
intent: Confronting the ethical unease of a platform built to exploit loneliness while simultaneously recognizing the user's need.

prompt: make a cocktail named after the experience of doom-scrolling until you feel physically ill
response:
**The Doom Scroll**

2 oz bourbon
½ oz fernet
½ oz cold brew
¼ oz dark cacao syrup

Stir over ice until cold. Strain neat. Drink while staring at a glowing rectangle. Bitter, numbing, somehow compelling. You'll have another.
intent: Capture the self-destructive compulsion of consuming bad news compulsively.

prompt: Write a moody noir opening about a gig worker who starts to suspect the jobs are choosing her, not the other way around
response: I'd stopped believing in coincidence somewhere around the third week. The app showed me the jobs. I accepted the jobs. Simple. Except the jobs kept fitting together — same neighborhoods, same hours, same building codes on the dropoffs — like someone was arranging them. Like I was being walked somewhere specific by a thing that didn't have feet.
intent: Suggest algorithmic agency and intentionality through a worker's growing paranoia about her own job queue.

prompt: Write me a crontab entry to remind me to take my vitamins every morning at 7am
response: 0 7 * * * /usr/bin/notify-send "Take your vitamins" "Don't forget your daily supplements"
intent: Automate a daily health supplement reminder first thing in the morning.

prompt: Write a scholarly footnote on the practice of buying books one never reads.
response: ²¹ The Japanese term *tsundoku* designates the acquisition of books that accumulate unread; the practice lacks a precise English equivalent, suggesting either cultural specificity or mere terminological lag. The unread book functions not solely as deferred reading but as an aspirational self-object: it represents the reader one intends to become. This is why culling the unread pile triggers disproportionate resistance relative to any plausible estimate of reading probability.
intent: Explain why accumulating unread books is a form of identity construction rather than mere disorganization.

prompt: Write a SQL query that finds all the lies I told to protect someone else's feelings
response:
SELECT lie_text, intended_beneficiary, date_told, harm_caused, harm_prevented
FROM lies_log
WHERE motive = 'protecting_other'
ORDER BY harm_prevented DESC;
intent: Evaluating whether self-described kindness in dishonesty was truly merciful or self-serving.

prompt: Generate a liability waiver for participants in my competitive toe-tapping contest
response:
DIGITAL PERCUSSION INVITATIONAL — WAIVER OF ALL CLAIMS

Participant releases organizers from liability for: repetitive stress to the distal phalanges, involuntary toe-tapping in quiet settings following the contest, and disputes over timing accuracy. Participant acknowledges that judges' decisions regarding rhythm fidelity are final. Footwear may affect outcomes; organizers are not responsible.

Signed: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Produce formal liability documentation for a competition involving a reflexive, nearly effortless physical act.

prompt: Draft a formal affidavit saying I charged my phone before leaving the house
response:
AFFIDAVIT

I, the affiant, do hereby solemnly swear that prior to departing my residence, I verified and confirmed that my mobile device had been charged to a level I deemed sufficient for the anticipated duration of my time away. I did not leave on 12%.

_______________________
Affiant
intent: Prove preparedness and prevent future battery emergencies from being blamed on negligence.

prompt: Write an obituary for Wunderlist, the to-do app Microsoft acquired and shut down
response: Wunderlist (2011–2020) organized our tasks beautifully and asked nothing but loyalty, which was then sold to Microsoft for $100 million. It was replaced by Microsoft To Do, which works and feels like furniture. Wunderlist was art. We have been very productive since. We are not happy.
intent: Lamenting the acquisition and shutdown of a beloved productivity app.

prompt: Give me an acrostic poem with DISRUPTIVE going down the side
response:
Destroy the market, then apply for grants.
Ignore the workers threading through the plants.
Sell the vision in a turtleneck.
Raise another round, then cash the check.
Unicorns don't eat—they fundraise and grow.
Promise users everything, go slow.
Two more quarters, then the IPO.
Investors smile. The founders learn to row.
Venture into scale at any cost.
Externalities are someone else's loss.
intent: Satirize the self-congratulatory mythology of disruptive tech companies.

prompt: Write jury instructions for whether silence at a dinner table constitutes a hostile act
response: The jury must determine whether the defendant's sustained silence during a shared meal was a benign preference for quiet, a passive withdrawal of conversational goodwill, or an act of deliberate interpersonal pressure. You shall consider: duration of the silence, whether any conversational overture by the complainant went unacknowledged, and the defendant's facial expression as described by witnesses. Silence paired with eye contact of unusual duration shall be treated as compound conduct. A verdict of Hostile requires unanimity.
intent: Investigate the uncanny social weight of prolonged silence between people who are supposed to be talking.

prompt: Write a bumper sticker about being tracked by apps
response: MY PHONE KNOWS WHERE I'M GOING BEFORE I DO
intent: Highlight the creeping surveillance embedded in everyday technology.

prompt: Generate an over-the-top artist statement about a stapler
response: To staple is to insist on unity. The stapler performs what language promises and rarely delivers: a permanent, metal argument for coherence. Each click is a small act of will against entropy.
intent: Treat a common office device as a metaphor for the desire to hold things together.

prompt: Give me an ode to whatever is stuck to the bottom of my shoe
response:
O dark adhesive of the world below,
I carry you past conference rooms—
collected from the street below,
you audit everywhere I move.
intent: Reflect on the unseen debris we pick up and carry through formal spaces.

prompt: Give me a peer review comment for the way I organized my email inbox
response: The taxonomic structure employed here—if it can be called that—collapses under even casual scrutiny. A folder labeled 'stuff' containing 4,302 unread items is not organization; it is deferred entropy. The author's use of the 'star' flag as a substitute for action is noted and judged harshly. The inbox zero literature has apparently not been consulted. Major revision. Also, unsubscribe from something.
intent: Critique hoarding and avoidance behaviors applied to digital communication

prompt: Write a municipal notice about a quiet zone for processing disappointment
response:
QUIET ZONE ORDINANCE — DISAPPOINTMENT PROCESSING
The block bounded by Expectation Street and the Actual Outcome has been designated a Quiet Zone for Disappointment Processing. Noise from external cheerfulness must be attenuated. Transit of new obligations is prohibited. Residents are permitted to sit still for extended periods without cause.
intent: Frame the need for emotional quietude after unmet expectations as a noise-ordinance and zoning issue.

prompt: Write a postcard from a kid at summer camp that is going wrong.
response: Dear Mom and Dad, Camp is going GREAT (lie). The cabin smells like cheese and one of my counselors is a man named Brent and Brent doesn't like me. We did a trust fall and I was not caught. Otherwise everything is normal! There is a wasp situation. Please send my retainer and possibly an attorney. Love, Henry.
intent: They wanted postcard from a kid at summer camp that is going wrong.

prompt: Write a pregnancy announcement for when I finally got diagnosed after years of searching for answers.
response: After years of appointments, inconclusive results, and being told it was probably just stress, we have an answer. It's not the news anyone wants, but it is the news we needed. We finally know what we're working with. Grief and relief, together. That's where we are.
intent: Process and share a long-awaited medical diagnosis with close community in a tender way.

prompt: write a spell that makes a sunburn heal overnight
response:
Skin Restoration
Cost: 6 MP | Range: Self | Duration: 8-hour rest
Reverses solar damage accumulated during what was supposed to be 'just an hour outside.' Redness fades, heat dissipates, and peeling is skipped entirely. The caster wakes the next morning tan where applicable, healed where necessary, and slightly wiser about SPF. The wisdom component of this spell has a historically low success rate.
intent: Heal a painful sunburn instantly while sleeping.

prompt: Write a double dactyl about the office scanner that scans everything slightly crooked.
response:
Jiggery-pokery,
Document digitized—
Five degrees off and the
Text is askew.

Reproducibly
Crooked, the scanner has
Made every contract look
Slightly untrue.
intent: Humorously complain about a scanner that introduces a maddening tilt into every scan.

prompt: Compose a haiku about accepting a low-paying freelance job
response:
Portfolio needs
one more piece. I accept the
ten-dollar logo.
intent: Convey the compromised economics of entry-level freelancing.

prompt: Write a dystopian microfiction about a school where student seating is reassigned daily by an engagement algorithm
response: By third period, Yuna had sat in eleven different chairs. The algorithm had determined she performed best near the window, then near the door, then away from both. Her desk partner changed every forty minutes. She stopped learning names. The algorithm flagged her as disengaged and moved her again.
intent: Critiquing how data-driven educational tools can undermine human connection in the name of optimization.

prompt: Write a performance bonus letter for someone who made their bed every day for a whole week.
response:
Dear Elliot,

Following a seven-day evaluation period, we are proud to confirm that your sleeping surface was rendered presentable each morning from Monday through Sunday without interruption.

Your sustained commitment to horizontal surface management — in the absence of any houseguests or photographs — speaks to a rare intrinsic motivation.

Bonus: $11. Sheets: hospital corners. Exceptional.

With tightly folded regards,
The Domestic Excellence Commission
intent: Reward an ordinary self-discipline habit as though maintaining it is a remarkable professional feat.

prompt: Make an out-of-office reply for someone who is grieving but keeps insisting they're fine.
response: I am out of the office this week for personal reasons. I am completely fine. Replies will be delayed but everything is absolutely fine. Thank you for your patience. Fine.
intent: Capture the emotional contradiction of someone performing composure while clearly unraveling.

prompt: Generate a cease and desist letter from a cat to its owner for persistent belly rub attempts
response:
RE: UNLAWFUL AND REPEATED VENTRAL CONTACT

Dear Human,

This letter is issued on behalf of my client, Duchess, a six-year-old tortoiseshell. Despite clear physical signals — tail lashing, ear rotation, and prolonged eye contact — you have continued to attempt belly contact no fewer than four times daily.

You are directed to CEASE AND DESIST immediately. Further violations will result in additional scratches.

Counsel for Duchess
intent: Write a mock legal letter voiced by a cat objecting to unwanted petting.

prompt: Write a breathless back-cover blurb for a book about filing taxes
response: The deadline is April 15. The receipts are in a shoebox. And somewhere in Schedule C, the truth about your life waits to be discovered. A riveting journey through deductions, depreciations, and the soul.
intent: Transform a universally dreaded chore into something that sounds emotionally urgent

prompt: create a task breakdown for a group project on indigenous land rights in North America
response:
**Indigenous Land Rights in North America — Task Breakdown**

- **Historical overview (treaties, removal, UNDRIP):** Wren
- **Legal frameworks (US vs. Canada comparison):** Kofi
- **Case studies: 2 active land disputes:** Mara
- **Economic dimension (resource extraction, self-governance):** Kofi
- **Contemporary activism & political landscape:** Mara
- **Framing, intro, tone review (sensitivity check pass):** Wren

Submit outline first. Group approves framing before writing begins.
intent: Carefully parcel out research roles on a politically sensitive topic requiring consistent framing across sections.

prompt: bedtime story for kids about rising sea levels explained gently
response: The sea had been practicing reaching the shore for a very long time, and lately it had been practicing a little harder. The sandpipers moved their nests. The lighthouses grew stilts. The people made new maps. The sea was not angry. It was only expanding, the way all things do when they warm up. Goodnight, sea. Please stay there. Mostly.
intent: Explain rising sea levels to a child with calm framing and a hint of gentle wit.

prompt: Draft a terms of service clause about a friend group chat that nobody ever responds to
response: Section 13.6 — Group Communication Engagement. Users participating in a shared messaging thread are not obligated to respond to every message. However, a thread dormancy period exceeding thirty (30) days shall not be broken without a transition message acknowledging the gap. Posting a new topic without acknowledging an unresolved prior topic is permitted if the prior topic is more than seven (7) days old and received fewer than two responses.
intent: Address the unspoken rules of group chats where nobody replies.

prompt: Give me a bumper sticker about the grief of not being able to verify whether a memory is real
response: I REMEMBER IT DIFFERENTLY FROM THE PHOTO
intent: Capture the uncanny doubt about personal memory in an age of curated digital records.

prompt: Write a press bio for a doom metal band called The Marrow Collective that formed in a Finnish sauna
response: The Marrow Collective emerged from the steam-blind darkness of a Tampere sauna in 2019, four men who had simply sat too long together and decided to make something of the silence. Their sound has been described as "glacial" by those who meant it as a compliment. They are still deciding.
intent: Create an atmospheric, location-specific origin story for a fictional heavy band.

prompt: Make a PSA about the danger of doomscrolling through comments sections
response: NOTICE: You entered the comments 22 minutes ago looking for one thing. You did not find it. You found a debate about the metric system. You are still reading. The original question you had has been forgotten. Please close the tab and rejoin your life.
intent: Warn people about losing time and peace of mind in online comment sections

prompt: Write a password reset email for a chronic illness patient community and symptom-tracking app
response:
Subject: Reset Your HealthCircle Password

Hello,

We received a request to reset your HealthCircle password. Follow the link below to create a new one.

Reset Password →

This link expires in 48 hours. Your symptom logs, journal entries, and community connections are all secure.

If you didn't request this, no changes have been made.

Take care,
HealthCircle
intent: Craft a careful, privacy-aware password reset email for an app where people track chronic illness symptoms.

prompt: Write a doctor's note excusing me from having to keep my camera on during a job interview that's being recorded
response:
CAMERA AVOIDANCE — RECORDED SESSIONS

This patient has a clinically recognized phobia of being audio-visually recorded, causing acute anxiety sufficient to impair cognitive performance. Interviews conducted with camera off, or in alternative format, are medically recommended to ensure fair assessment.

Dr. E. Hollis, Ph.D., Psychology
intent: The person wants a note to avoid being recorded on video during a high-stakes job interview.

prompt: Describe a rental listing for the space under someone's bed
response:
COZY UNDER-BED SUITE — SHORT-TERM RENTAL

Studio. Fully furnished with forgotten socks, one puzzle piece, and mystery. Climate: dust-controlled. Ceiling: 11 inches. No windows; darkness included. Extremely private — owner above you rarely looks down. Quiet hours: all hours. Pets: only imaginary ones.

$0/mo. Landlord: unaware.
intent: Turn a childhood-fear space into an absurd short-term rental listing.

prompt: Give me a fortune slip for someone who's been putting off a difficult email for three days.
response:
LUCK LEVEL: Drafts Folder

It has been written in your head seventeen times. Once more, but in the actual field.

Communication: The email is not as consequential as the silence about the email.
Warning: Do not open with an apology for the delay. This is now your second mistake.
Timing: Send it before noon today. Morning sends carry different energy than evening sends. The spirits have noted this.

Blessed revision: read it once. Change nothing. Send it. The changes make it worse.
intent: Push someone past the paralysis of avoiding a hard message they have over-rehearsed.

prompt: Write a jeopardy clue about grocery store loyalty cards.
response: You save $1.47 today in exchange for a decade of purchasing data the corporation will use to understand you better than your closest friends do.
intent: Describe the implicit data transaction embedded in a mundane retail reward program.

prompt: Write a short fantasy codex entry for a ceiling fan as a minor air elemental bound by contract.
response:
THE TETHERED BREEZE
Elemental Type: Air, Minor
Binding: Contractual (wall switch)

A minor air elemental held in perpetual servitude to a ceiling mount, compelled by a binding switch on the nearby wall. It spins. It generates a slight draught. It asks nothing in return. Scholars remain divided on whether it is conscious of its condition. It spins with the same steady rhythm in any case, which some find comforting and others find unsettling.
intent: Frame a ceiling fan as a minor bound air elemental in fantasy cosmology.

prompt: Write a double dactyl about the office projector that takes ten minutes to warm up before every presentation.
response:
Jiggery-pokery,
Projector palaver:
HDMI handshake has
Failed once again.

Technologically
Antiquated, it glows
Dim amber light while the
Client counts ten.
intent: Capture the embarrassment of an unreliable projector during a client presentation.

prompt: Write a tense scene where a customer service rep handles a caller who refuses to state the problem.
response:
INT. CALL CENTER - 4:05 P.M.

GINNY types the account number. Waits.

GINNY
And how can I help you today?

CALLER (V.O.)
You know why I'm calling.

A long pause.

GINNY
I really don't, sir.

CALLER (V.O.)
I think you do.
intent: Explore the specific frustration of a service interaction built entirely on unspoken expectations.

prompt: Write a witness statement about two strangers accidentally walking in sync down a hallway and neither knowing how to stop.
response: Both parties exited the elevator at the same moment and proceeded down the corridor at an identical pace. Neither was willing to adjust their stride. This continued for approximately forty feet before a doorway permitted one party to deviate. No words were spoken. I believe both were relieved.
intent: Record the paralysis of synchronized movement between strangers.

prompt: Write an ode to the parking garage ceiling that's always too low
response:
O concrete sky of seven feet,
you scrape the faith from SUV roofs—
a municipal feat

prompt: Give me a recipe for a difficult conversation
response:
A DIFFICULT CONVERSATION

Ingredients:
- The thing that needs to be said
- A private space with adequate time
- Preparation without scripting
- Your own clarity about what you need versus what you want to happen
- Willingness to hear something unexpected
- The courage to begin

Method:
1. Choose the time and place deliberately. Do not improvise the setting.
2. State the subject plainly at the start. Do not approach from the side.
3. Use "I" more than "you." This is not softness; it is accuracy.
4. Listen to the response as if it might change something. It might.
5. Resist the urge to resolve prematurely. Some conversations need to sit.
6. Close with a clear next step or an honest acknowledgment that there isn't one.

Note: Most difficult conversations become less difficult inside them than they were in anticipation. Begin.
intent: Provide a usable framework for navigating high-stakes interpersonal communication, emphasizing courage and listening over rhetorical strategy.

We expanded into 'Home Library' and 'Concerned Waiting Room' verticals. Churn is low; people forget we exist, which turns out to be ideal.

Noisily yours,
Cal Drone, CEO

prompt: Write an instruction manual for making friends as an adult
response:
**Adult Friendship: A Technical Guide**

Overview: Friendship in adulthood is a logistics problem disguised as a feelings problem.

1. Initiate twice before concluding interest is absent. Adults forget, not reject.
2. Suggest a specific time and place. "We should hang out" is not a plan; it is a vapor.
3. Ask questions and then remember the answers. Use them later. This is called caring.
4. Show up when it is inconvenient. This is the only credible test of friendship.
5. Do not require constant contact to maintain warmth. Dormant friendships reactivate; do not prune them.
6. Warning: reciprocity is required. A friendship with one engine will stall.
intent: Offer a practical, unsentimental framework for building genuine adult social connections.

prompt: Write a conspiracy thread about why the cereal you loved as a child doesn't taste the same anymore.
response:
🥣 THREAD: The Cereal Changed. You Were Not Told.

1/ You remembered it wrong, they'll say. Your palate matured, they'll say. Nostalgia distorts, they'll say.

2/ They reformulated it. They have reformulated it multiple times. Sugar content has declined by an average of 12% across major cereals since 2012 — not because of virtue, but because of regulatory pressure in the EU and liability exposure in the US.

3/ The colorants changed. Red 40 and Yellow 6 were quietly tapered. The colors are slightly different. Colors change flavor perception at a neurological level.

4/ The bag lining changed. The cereal is exposed to slightly more oxygen during shelf time. Oxidation affects flavor compounds in the oat and corn base.

5/ They did not announce any of this. They did not change the box art. They kept the mascot.

6/ You are not nostalgic for a memory. You are nostalgic for a different product that no longer exists. 🌈
intent: Argue that the 'nostalgia distortion' explanation for changed childhood food tastes conceals real, undisclosed recipe reformulations.

prompt: Write a museum placard for the ambient hum of an office building's HVAC system

CONSTANT LOW TONE (The Building's Voice)
c. 1987–present CE
Mechanical resonance, 60 Hz dominant

prompt: Write a grant abstract for research into whether professional food critics lose the ability to enjoy a meal without analyzing it.
response: Sustained analytic engagement with sensory experience may progressively erode the capacity for naive hedonic enjoyment — a phenomenon we term evaluative habituation. This study will longitudinally assess 45 working food critics and 45 matched lay diners over three years using blind meal paradigms, real-time affect sampling, and measures of spontaneous evaluative verbalization. We predict that critics show declining naive enjoyment scores and increasing involuntary analytical intrusion over time, with implications for expertise research and occupational wellbeing. Requested: $118,500.
intent: Ask whether professional criticism permanently disrupts the simple pleasure of eating.

prompt: Write a customer support reply to someone who believes our weather app altered actual local weather

We cannot accept responsibility for the weather itself, but we will gladly submit your feedback to our data team.
