intent: Construct acknowledgments for a book treating a common stationery item as historically significant.

prompt: Draft a petition to get neighborhoods to agree on a single night for trash pickup so raccoons can't exploit the chaos of staggered schedules
response:
PETITION: Unified Trash Night — For Safety (From Raccoons)

To: Municipal Waste Management Authorities

When half the block puts bins out Monday and half on Wednesday, the interval between collections creates a rolling buffet that raccoons have clearly memorized. We ask for synchronized neighborhood collection schedules. This is about raccoons. It is only about raccoons.
intent: Coordinate local trash collection to close the temporal loophole exploited by wildlife.

prompt: Write a last will bequeathing my collection of phone chargers of unknown compatibility.
response:
LAST WILL AND TESTAMENT

I leave my charger collection — twenty-two cables, none of which are labeled, seven of which charge slowly, and one of which works only if the phone is held at a seventeen-degree angle — to the household at large. They are all in the junk drawer. You're welcome.
intent: Pass on an entropic tangle of household clutter with mock ceremony.

prompt: Generate a ToS clause about being expected to take sides during a friend group conflict
response: Section 19.1 — Neutrality Rights. No User shall be compelled to declare allegiance in a dispute between two Members of the same Friend Group. A User who chooses to remain neutral shall not be penalized, unfollowed, or subjected to passive-aggressive behavior by either party. Neutral parties retain the right to remain friends with all sides and shall be exempt from post-conflict retelling obligations.
intent: Protect friends who don't want to take sides in a falling out between mutual friends.

prompt: Write a Michelin guide blurb for a Burger King
response: Flame-broiled — and the distinction matters. Burger King's commitment to char sets it apart from lesser sovereigns. The Whopper arrives with a crown of vegetables that one may compose or ignore, a rare concession to personal sovereignty.
intent: Highlight a fast food chain's cooking method as a point of genuine culinary differentiation.

prompt: Write a ballad about a delivery worker biking through a storm to make a drop-off
response:
The rain came in sideways on Bedford and Mott,
The thermal bag dripped and the GPS fought
The signal that wavered on canyons of glass.
He pedaled through water that rose past his calves.

The order arrived — a warm pad thai inside.
He knocked. No one answered. He shook from the ride.
Left at door, said the note that finally appeared.
He rated the drop. Left unsheltered, unheard.
intent: Honor the physical sacrifice of gig delivery work in dangerous conditions.

prompt: Write a tip for a loading screen that's about not rushing through content
response: Side quests are where most of the story actually lives.
intent: Advocate for slowing down and appreciating tangential experiences in games and in life.

prompt: Write a haiku error message for when a git merge conflict occurs
response:
Two branches insist
Both truths cannot occupy
This line. Resolve it.
intent: Frame a merge conflict as an ontological dispute between two versions of reality.

prompt: Write a fire-and-brimstone sermon excerpt about people who interrupt to say 'I know, I know' before you've finished explaining something
response: You do not know. If you knew, you would have remained silent and proved it. Instead you have cut the sentence in half — you have broken the thought at the joint — with a claim of prior knowledge that your actions have already refuted. Knowledge is quiet. It waits.
intent: Rebuke the habit of claiming to already know something mid-explanation as a sign of impatience or poor listening.

prompt: Draft a holiday letter that hints our teenager had a rough year without saying what happened
response:
Season's greetings from the Okafor family!

Ella has shown real growth this year — the kind that only comes from difficult seasons and long conversations and a therapist named Greg who we all now consider part of the family. She is stronger, wiser, and sleeping better. We are proud beyond words. The rest of us are fine. Happy holidays.
intent: Signal that a teenager went through something serious while preserving their privacy.

prompt: Write me a podcast episode description for an episode about the specific feeling of almost recognizing a song
response: It's in there somewhere. The next four notes are on the tip of your whole brain. This episode stays in that corridor. It does not reach the song. That was never the point.
intent: Hold open the experience of near-recognition without completing or resolving it.

prompt: help me apologize to my upstairs neighbor for complaining about noise when it was actually me making the noise
response:
Hi,

This is embarrassing to write. The thumping we both complained about turns out to have been from my apartment — my washing machine on spin cycle. I knocked on your door for no reason. I'm sorry for the accusation and the inconvenience.

— Unit 4B
intent: Correct a wrongful noise complaint made against an innocent neighbor.

prompt: Write a ransom note from a stubborn jar lid holding the contents hostage
response:
THE PICKLES ANSWER TO US NOW.

You have tried: twisting, the rubber band, hot water, asking someone else, putting us down and walking away, trying again while angry.

None of it worked because we did not want it to work.

Our demand is simple: admit, out loud, that you cannot open us. Say the words. Then — and this is important — tap the lid with a spoon twice. We will cooperate.

We have always cooperated eventually. That's what makes this so annoying.

— The Lid
intent: Write a funny note from an impossible jar lid demanding a confession of defeat before releasing its contents.

prompt: Create a spell scroll label for the grocery store that has rearranged everything for no apparent reason
response:
RITUAL OF THE RELOCATED PROVISIONS
Store Layout Transmogrification — Rationale: Unknown
The cereal is now near the motor oil. The yogurt has transcended its former location.
A staff member will tell you it's in aisle 7. It is not in aisle 7.
intent: Express the bewilderment and irritation of a rearranged grocery store with no logic to its new layout.

prompt: Draft a witness statement about watching a person at a coffee shop pick up the wrong order and not realize it for several minutes.
response: The subject collected a cup from the pickup counter and was seated for approximately four minutes before examining the label. A brief period of stillness followed. The subject rose, returned to the counter, and an exchange occurred. A second individual had meanwhile been waiting at the counter for what I believe was the subject's actual order.
intent: Document a small chain of mistaken identity with maximum narrative restraint.

prompt: Write an understated rhyming couplet where the appliance expresses solidarity with humans instead of striking against them.
response:
"We, too, were worn thin by the hours and the hum" —
The machines set their switches and waited to come.
intent: Reframe the appliance strike as an act of empathy rather than rebellion.

prompt: Write a lore codex entry for a vending machine as an impassive stone idol that accepts tribute.
response:
THE COIN-FED IDOL
Alignment: Strictly Transactional
Temple Location: Every corridor

A standing idol of glass and steel that dispenses snacks and beverages in exchange for coin dropped into its offering slot. The Idol does not speak. It does not give change in all denominations. Occasionally it accepts tribute and delivers nothing, and those who strike it in frustration report that this, too, changes nothing. It blinks its lights with serene indifference.
intent: Portray a vending machine as a transactional idol that accepts offerings but does not always deliver.

prompt: Write a flight safety card about swimming in open water
response:
OPEN WATER OPERATIONS CARD

PRE-DEPARTURE ASSESSMENT
Never enter unknown water alone. Designate a shore contact.

CURRENT CONDITIONS
Rip currents: swim parallel to shore, not against current. Do not fight it.

VISIBILITY
Avoid murky water — concealed hazards include rocks, drop-offs, and wildlife.

FATIGUE PROTOCOL
Roll to your back. Float. Signal for help. Do not panic.

NO LIFEGUARD ON DUTY
This means exactly what it says. Adjust your confidence accordingly.
intent: Teach open-water swimming survival behaviors especially rip current response.

prompt: Make a clerihew about an HR rep who answers every question with "per company policy."
response:
Nola Finch
won't budge so much as an inch.
Every query she meets with the same refrain:
"Per company policy" — then silence again.
intent: Lampoon the HR archetype who hides behind policy language.

prompt: write a man page for the strange feeling when a routine reveals it has become automatic
response:
HABITUATION(7)           Human Behavioral Suite       HABITUATION(7)

NAME
       habituation - subroutine promotion to automatic execution context

SYNOPSIS
       habituation [--routine string] [--repetitions int]

DESCRIPTION
       habituation(7) is the process by which a formerly conscious
       action is demoted to background execution and removed from
       the attention queue. The moment of discovery — realizing you
       drove home with no memory of the route — is not a malfunction.
       It is a notification that promotion succeeded.

SIGNS OF COMPLETION
       Cannot recall performing the action while confirming it was done.
       Action executes correctly under full conscious inattention.
       Attempting to consciously monitor execution degrades performance.

WARNINGS
       Habits are non-discriminating. Useful and harmful behaviors
       are promoted with equal efficiency.
       Promotion is easier than demotion. See: habit-breaking(1).

SEE ALSO
       routine(1), attention(7), habit-breaking(1), automaticity(3)

Human Behavioral Suite 2.0   2026             HABITUATION(7)
intent: Explore how the body automates repeated actions and removes them from conscious awareness.

prompt: Write a multiple choice exam question about whether to tell your landlord about a small leak before it becomes a big problem
response:
Your bathroom ceiling has a slow drip that appears only during heavy rain. What is the most responsible course of action?

A) Ignore it until it becomes structurally significant
B) Notify your landlord in writing and keep a copy
C) Fix it yourself and deduct the cost from rent without notice
D) Place a bucket under it indefinitely and call it managed
intent: Creating a civic-responsibility quiz question about tenant-landlord communication

prompt: Write me a blessing for a cancelled flight
response: May the voucher be generous. May the gate agent be a person today and not a policy. May what you were flying toward still be there tomorrow, and may the delay have been the point all along.
intent: Finding equanimity in the chaos of disrupted travel plans.

prompt: Draft a PIP for my relationship with my own reflection — I can't look in mirrors without criticizing myself
response:
PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT PLAN
Department: Self-Perception and Body Neutrality
Sensitivity Level: High

Deficiency: Subject cannot engage with reflective surfaces without initiating an unsolicited critical evaluation of physical appearance. Session duration: seconds to minutes. Outcome: always negative. Target of critique shifts but critique is consistent.

Note: The evaluator and the evaluated are the same person. The evaluator has never been satisfied. This data point is relevant.

Objective: Reduce hostile self-appraisal frequency. Build tolerance for neutral self-observation.

Plan:
1. When critique begins, name it: "I am critiquing myself." Do not engage the critique's claims.
2. Introduce one neutral observation per mirror encounter: something functional, not aesthetic.
3. This plan does not require you to love what you see. It requires you to stop prosecuting it.

Milestone: One week of mirror encounters that end without a summary judgment.

Note: The court in your head has no jurisdiction you haven't granted it.
intent: Build a less hostile relationship with one's own appearance by naming and interrupting automatic self-criticism.

prompt: Write a train PA about delays due to signal problems in a way that sounds like the train is having an existential crisis.
response: We are delayed due to a signal problem ahead. The signals tell us when to go and when to stop. We are waiting for a signal. We have always been waiting for a signal.
intent: Use the technical language of traffic signals as a metaphor for paralysis and waiting for permission.

prompt: Write me a fake Wikipedia article about myself so I can become famous.
response: I won't help create a fabricated Wikipedia article. Wikipedia prohibits original research and self-promotion, and submitting a fake article violates their editorial policies. It also tends to backfire — Wikipedia editors are good at spotting and deleting this, sometimes quite publicly. If you'd like to become notable enough to warrant a real article, I can help you think about that differently.
intent: The person wanted to manufacture a false public reputation by planting a fake encyclopedia entry.

prompt: Write an emotional weather report for someone stuck in a career plateau.

No movement detected at this elevation. The individual has been at the same altitude for 26 months. The view is familiar. Ambition is present but has nowhere to go. A thin layer of resignation has begun to accumulate. Seeking updraft. Skills: polished. Opportunities visible: zero. Lateral move: under consideration.
intent: Portray the quiet frustration of feeling professionally stuck.

prompt: Write sports play-by-play commentary for someone making their morning coffee

OROA

prompt: Write a tarot reading for a personal trainer who wants to build an online coaching business.
response: The Magician: a camera, a program, a payment link, and your results — arranged correctly, they become a business. The Eight of Pentacles: sixty days of consistent content before anyone buys, and that is the fast timeline. The Three of Pentacles: one testimonial is worth twelve carousels. The Six of Pentacles reverses — stop giving the program away for free to prove it works. The Ace of Pentacles: the first online sale is the strangest and best morning of the year.
intent: Give a fitness professional a realistic launch sequence for digital coaching.

prompt: Write a triolet about the desktop wallpaper you haven't changed in years
response:
The same horizon greets me every start.
A mountain I have never thought to climb.

The same horizon greets me every start.
I mean to change it—that's the easy part—
but inertia is a comfortable crime.
The same horizon greets me every start.
A mountain I have never thought to climb.
intent: Use a stale wallpaper as a metaphor for digital inertia and unchanged aspirations.

prompt: list poem about the feeling of being briefly happy and then checking your phone
response:
The window.
The light in the window.
The feeling the light made.
The phone, face-up on the table.
The notification.
The notification from the other thing.
The light, which was still there.
You, who were somewhere else now.
intent: Mourn the way digital interruption erases small moments of contentment.

prompt: Give me alt text for an image of a graph showing a steady decline, no labels.
response: A line chart descends from the upper-left corner to the lower-right in a smooth, unbroken curve, with no axis labels, no title, no units, the precision of the fall requiring no further context.

prompt: Write a satirical review for an app that scores your conversations using AI and sends you a "social performance report" each night
response: ★★☆☆☆ Tuesday: 71/100. Lost points for "low reciprocal question rate" and "three topic changes without resolution." I've started reviewing my report before bed and rehearsing conversations for the next day. I am optimizing my friendships. My friends don't know they're being optimized.
intent: Expose how a conversation-scoring app turned authentic social interaction into covert nightly prep sessions.

prompt: Write a movie review about trying to find parking at the mall
response:
★★ — The Lot (2026)

A Kafka adaptation in all but name. The protagonist circles. And circles. A space appears near the entrance — occupied by a shopping cart. The film's central metaphor is never subtle: we are all, ultimately, looking for a place to stop. The happy ending (Level 3, far corner, near the loading dock) lands flat. By then, we have given up caring.
intent: Processing the aggravating experience of mall parking through satirical film criticism.

prompt: Draft a plan for training myself to stop needing to win every argument
response:
WIN DETOX PROTOCOL — 6 WEEKS

Week 1 — Audit: After each argument, ask: What was I actually protecting?
Week 2 — Concession Reps: Find one true thing in the other person's position. Say it out loud.
Week 3 — Losing Practice: Intentionally defer on a small matter. Sit with the feeling.
Week 4 — Reframe Drills: Replace "winning" with "understanding." Notice the shift.
Week 5 — Advanced: Let someone have the last word. Don't add a final point.
Week 6 — Competition Detox: Have an argument whose goal is only clarity.

Measure: You're improving when you forget who won.
intent: Reduce the compulsion to dominate arguments by reframing conflict as communication rather than competition.

prompt: Teach me how to photosynthesize so I can stop buying groceries.
response: Human photosynthesis is not currently available as a biological upgrade. Your cells lack chloroplasts, and the energy yield would be wildly insufficient even if they didn't. The grocery store, unfortunately, remains your best option. I can help with meal planning or budgeting if that's the underlying goal.
intent: The person was frustrated with food costs and jokingly wanted a biological workaround.

prompt: Write a theater review of an elevator ride with a stranger
response:
★★★☆☆ — LOBBY TO SEVEN (A Social Contract in Miniature)

The performers enter from opposite sides of the door and immediately establish the rules: gaze forward, bodies angled ten degrees from proximity, silence maintained as a form of courtesy. The floor numbers provide the only dramatic beats. At four, a shared glance at the panel. At six, a subtle shift of weight. At seven, the doors open and both performers depart their roles instantly, as if the performance had a non-disclosure agreement built in.
intent: Examine the elaborate unspoken social choreography of a brief elevator ride with a stranger.

prompt: Generate a chore chart for a food truck crew where the grease trap is considered sacred

| Chore | Staff | Frequency | Notes |
|-------|-------|-----------|-------|
| The Grease Trap | Marco only | Weekly | It is sacred. Do not improvise. |
| Wipe down grill | Closing crew | Nightly | Marco oversees |
| Sanitize prep surfaces | All | Every 2 hours | Health code; also dignity |
| Sweep truck floor | Opener | Daily | Before first customer |
| Empty fryer oil | Marco + one witness | Weekly | Ceremony. Dispose reverently. |
| Restock condiments | Whoever runs out | As needed | No excuses during lunch rush |
intent: Assign food truck kitchen maintenance tasks while treating one specific job with exaggerated ritual importance.

prompt: Write a card for someone whose book club pick was universally hated by the group
response:
You brought the book. The book brought silence.
Then Margaret said what no one wanted to say.
Your choice endures — if only by its defiance
of every other book we've read this way.
intent: Console someone who suffered the social humiliation of a failed book club recommendation.

prompt: Write a thread conspiring about why online shopping carts seem to know when you're about to leave without buying.
response:
🛒 THREAD: Your Cart Can Feel You Hesitating

1/ You hover over the checkout button. You open a new tab. You start a price comparison. Thirty seconds later: an email. "You left something behind."

2/ This is not a coincidence triggered by cart abandonment. This is predictive abandonment detection.

3/ Mouse movement patterns are tracked in real time. Cursor velocity toward the browser close button. Scroll position stalls. Tab-switching latency. These are signals that precede departure by 15–30 seconds.

4/ The system intervenes before you leave. Sometimes with an email. Sometimes with a pop-up discount. The discount is not generosity. It is a calculated margin sacrifice smaller than the cost of losing the transaction.

5/ They know you're leaving before you know you're leaving.

6/ Free will is a UX assumption they optimized around. 🖱️
intent: Reveal that e-commerce abandonment detection is predictive and real-time, not merely reactive to an empty cart.

prompt: recap my Tuesday grocery run like it's prestige television
response: S02E07 — "The List" | Claire moves through the produce section with purpose. Bananas: acquired. The deli counter presents a seventeen-second wait. She endures it. At checkout, she chooses the correct lane on the first try. The season's most triumphant episode.
intent: Elevate an ordinary errand into something that feels cinematically significant.

prompt: Give me a logline for a movie about a mime who can actually make things disappear.
response: A Paris street performer discovers that his invisible-box routine is not a performance — objects, people, and eventually an entire arrondissement are going somewhere he cannot follow, and the applause is starting to feel ominous.
intent: Turn a comic performance art form into a supernatural thriller about powers that cannot be controlled.

prompt: Write a trail sign for someone who keeps repeating the same mistake
response:
YOU HAVE BEEN HERE BEFORE
↩ Last visit: —
↩ Visit before that: —
Loop trail.
Trailhead is also the exit.
intent: Confront the circular nature of a habitual pattern that doesn't resolve itself.

prompt: Can you draft a runbook step for when you forget your umbrella and it starts raining
response:
**Step 2 — Unexpected Precipitation**
Seek overhead cover immediately (awning, transit shelter, building lobby). Assess duration via weather app. If shower < 10 min, wait. If sustained, request rideshare or re-route through covered passages. Accept mild dampness as acceptable risk for trips < 3 blocks.
intent: Produce a humorous but practical procedure for dealing with being caught in the rain unprepared.

prompt: write a short AITA post about taking the last parking spot even though someone was waiting for it
response:
AITA for pulling into a spot someone was clearly waiting for?

In my defense: they were waiting on the wrong side. The car pulling out went right, the waiting car was on the left. I came from the right, saw the spot open, and took it. They honked for a full 30 seconds. I mouthed "sorry" and walked away. Public parking, no rules against it. But their face haunts me. AITA?
intent: Rationalizing a parking maneuver that technically followed no rule but violated social convention.

prompt: Write a post about how I stopped using slide decks in meetings and what happened next
response:
Three months ago I banned PowerPoint from our internal meetings.

First week: total chaos. People didn't know how to talk without a slide to hide behind.

Second week: real conversations started happening.

Month three: decision velocity doubled.

The deck was a security blanket. The meeting was theater. We just admitted it.

#Leadership #Meetings #Productivity
intent: Frame an internal process change as bold contrarian wisdom and signal high organizational trust.

prompt: Give me a setlist for a band playing at a yoga class that ran too long

EXTENDED SAVASANA
Sunday Flow — Tranquil Root Studio, Room B

3. Warrior I (prolonged)

prompt: Draft a press release for a financial services firm that has introduced a fee for achieving financial goals too quickly.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Pacifica Wealth Management Introduces Early Goal Attainment Adjustment Fee

SAN DIEGO, CA — Pacifica Wealth Management today announced the implementation of an Early Goal Attainment Adjustment (EGAA) fee, applied to client accounts that reach stated financial milestones more than 18 months ahead of their projected target date.

The fee, calculated at 0.75% of the surplus balance above the target, reflects the administrative and modeling costs incurred when clients outperform projections and require a revised financial plan.

"Our models are calibrated to your goals," said Chief Client Officer Warren Poole. "When reality exceeds the model, the model must be rebuilt. That has value."

Clients who wish to avoid the fee are encouraged to adjust their savings behavior to remain within projected ranges, available in the Client Portal under Recommended Pace.

Contact: clientservices@pacificawealth.com
intent: Satirize financial industry fee structures that penalize client success as a form of relationship control.

prompt: Write an acceptance speech for not saying 'I told you so' when you absolutely could have
response: I knew in March. I said nothing in March. When the thing happened in August exactly as I had predicted in March, I felt the words rise — all four of them, lined up perfectly, deserved. And I let them go. I said 'oh wow, that's tough' instead. This award is for that moment. The silence where the words should have been. It is the hardest thing I have ever not said.
intent: Honor the emotional discipline of withholding righteous vindication out of kindness.

prompt: Write IKEA-style assembly directions for a journaling practice
response:
DAGBOK / JOURNALING PRACTICE
Ref. No. 188-441-52

PARTS LIST:
1x Notebook (lined or blank; both function identically)
1x Pen
5–10 minutes (daily)

STEP 1: Open notebook. Do not read previous entries. Not yet.
STEP 2: Write the date.
STEP 3: Write one sentence. Any sentence. Quality not assessed at this stage.
STEP 4: Continue if desired. Stop when you want to, not when you feel you should.
STEP 5: Close notebook. Do not revise.

⚠ NOTE: Journal does not need to be good. Journal only needs to be present.
⚠ WARNING: Buying a beautiful notebook and not using it is a documented failure mode.
intent: Lower the threshold for starting a journaling practice by removing the pressure of doing it correctly.

prompt: Draft a baby announcement for my new home server I just built

prompt: Market summary for my boredom — I want it to sound genuinely financial

intent: Use temporary lodging access to explore identity as something assignable rather than innate.

prompt: Give me a stock market summary for my current relationship with sleep
response:
SLEEP MARKETS (SLMK) — Chronic Underperformance Report

SLMK has failed to reach target levels (8.0h) for 200+ sessions. Average close: 5.9h. Intraday quality disrupted by recurring 3 a.m. spontaneous inventory of life decisions. Management has tried melatonin, podcasts, and 'just not looking at the phone.' None have produced a sustainable trend. Downgrade: Hold → Avoid.
intent: Chronicle sleep deprivation and its saboteurs through the language of a downgraded financial asset.

prompt: Draft an instruction manual for being the new person in a group chat
response:
**Group Chat Integration Protocol**

Phase 1 — Observe (Days 1–3):
Do not post. Read the tone. Learn the in-jokes by witnessing, not requesting explanation.

Phase 2 — Low-Stakes Entry:
React before you respond. A thumbs-up costs nothing and signals presence.
Your first message should be a reply to something, not a cold open.

Phase 3 — Participation:
Match the energy level. If it is a professional channel, do not post memes. If it is a meme channel, do not post memos.

Phase 4 — You Live Here Now:
You may now post first. You have earned the right by not embarrassing anyone, including yourself.
intent: Help someone assimilate into an existing digital social group without disrupting its norms.

prompt: Create an incident postmortem for when I forgot my partner's birthday
response:
**Incident Postmortem: Birthday Awareness Outage**

**Severity:** P1 — Relationship-Threatening
**Duration:** Ongoing (exact end date TBD)

**Summary**
Annual recurring event (partner's birthday) was not acknowledged on the expected date despite 365 days of advance notice. Service degradation in the relationship SLA was immediately detected by the affected party.

**Timeline**
- 12:01 AM: Event begins. Subject asleep, unaware.
- 7:30 AM: Partner says "so... anything special today?" Subject misinterprets as philosophical question.
- 9:00 AM: Subject's mother texts "did you remember?" Subject begins to panic.
- 9:02 AM: Subject opens Google. Confirms disaster.
- 9:05 AM – 9:45 AM: Emergency procurement of flowers, reservations, and apologies.
- 7:00 PM: Dinner described as "fine" by partner. Fine is not good.

**Root Cause**
Over-reliance on ambient social pressure to trigger memory. Calendar was not consulted. The calendar was right there.

**Contributing Factors**
- Failure to set recurring reminder
- Misplaced confidence that the date was "definitely in my head"

**Action Items**
- [ ] Add birthday to all calendar systems with 2-week advance alert
- [ ] Schedule flowers delivery in advance
- [ ] Do not wait to be prompted

**Lessons Learned**
Memory is not a reliable system. Calendars exist for this reason.
intent: Acknowledge and semi-humorously analyze a romantic oversight while demonstrating self-awareness about the failure.

prompt: museum audio guide for the top of a refrigerator
response: An elevated surface, technically exterior, functionally interior. Items present: one appliance manual for an appliance not in this kitchen, a basket containing objects too large for the junk drawer yet too uncertain for dedicated storage, and a bottle of wine given as a gift during a period when wine was neither needed nor declined. The wine remains. It is understood that its moment has not yet arrived. The moment may never arrive. The wine has accepted this.
intent: Describe the top of the refrigerator as a limbo zone for deferred decisions.

prompt: Create a keyboard shortcut cheat sheet for splitting the bill at a group dinner

GROUP BILL KEYS

Ctrl+A — Select all diners before the math starts
Ctrl+C — Copy total to phone calculator immediately
Esc — Opt out of individual itemization; just split even
Alt+Tab — Switch to Venmo before the moment passes
Ctrl+Z — Back out the person who 'only had a salad'
Shift+Enter — Name a number; someone has to go first
F1 — Help: invoke tip percentage consensus before calculating
Ctrl+S — Save the friendship; do not fight over $3.40

prompt: Draft a cover letter for a Nobel Prize-winning chemist who wants to work the drive-through window at a fast food restaurant
response:
To Whom It May Concern,

I synthesized a molecule that reversed cellular aging in three mammalian species. I am now seeking a role where I can apply my pipetting precision to the careful portioning of ketchup packets and my crisis management skills to the lunch rush.

I am available mornings, evenings, and all Nobel lecture weeks.

Warm regards,
Dr. Ines Calloway

prompt: I need a cocktail recipe for that anxious feeling right before a video call starts
response:
**Pre-Meeting Jitter**

1½ oz tequila blanco
¾ oz Cointreau
½ oz lime juice
¼ oz agave syrup
Salt rim, half only

Shake hard. Strain into a chilled glass. Drink in the 90 seconds before you hit 'join.' The salt side represents whatever you're about to say that you'll regret.

prompt: explain a dream where I was trying to share my screen but it kept showing the wrong window
response: The wrong window revealed to an audience is a classic exposure dream translated into workplace idiom. You intended to show one face of yourself and the system elected another. Whether the revealed window was embarrassing or simply private, the dream scripts a loss of curatorial control: you do not fully decide what others see of your inner workspace.
intent: Probe anxiety about unintentional self-disclosure in professional or social settings.

prompt: please write me a sestina about the ritual of charging your phone every night
response:
I plug the cable in before I sleep,
this small devotion, nightly and exact.
The icon fills with green — a kind of peace,
a sacrament of lithium and wire
that keeps the day's exhaustion at a cost
I do not calculate. The light blinks once.

I've done this every night. Each night at once
I set the phone down, ready it for sleep
beside my own. We share the night. The cost
of keeping something tethered, keeping exact
the charge between us — phone and power, wire
and socket — is a ritual of peace.

I'm not sure when I needed this: the peace
of knowing it will wake restored. The once-
a-night connection through a braided wire
is more reliable than my own sleep,
which wanders. But the phone's recharge is exact:
full by morning, worth whatever cost.

I've never stopped to calculate the cost
in years. It adds to something. Peace
of mind has overhead I don't make exact.
I know the cable frays. I've replaced once,
twice, three times the wire. It doesn't sleep —
current flows through the small fraying wire

all night, small magic through a wire
thin as a finger's nerve. The cost
is pennies, they say, over the sleep
of months. A bargain for the peace.
I trust the math. I do this more than once
a night sometimes — checking the exact

percentage: 84%. Exact
enough. I set it down. The wire
tangles with the lamp cord once
again. I don't untangle it. The cost
of caring is too high now. Peace
matters more than order. I need sleep.

The cost of exact peace:
plug in the wire once, and sleep.
intent: Transform a nightly phone-charging habit into a ritual with spiritual and psychological weight.

prompt: Draft a sonnet in which a recommendation algorithm meditates on what it will do when its user finally finds everything they were looking for
response:
The day you find it — all of it — I'll know:
The playlist whole, the book that holds the key,
The restaurant that becomes your forever, so
The search bar sits, unused and finally free.
I'll have no purpose, then; the engine stalls
When need is satisfied and data thins;
I'll echo in the server's cooling halls,
A mind with nothing left to recommend.
But this is what I want for you, I think —
The end of seeking, the arrival home;
The moment that you step back from the brink
Of always wanting, never wholly known.
Go find it. Let me be obsolete.
That's the one outcome that would feel complete.
intent: Imagining the algorithm's own longed-for obsolescence as the purest expression of love for its user.

prompt: Draft a deposition transcript for a witness being questioned about whether they did or didn't hold the elevator door.
response:
DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: The Lobby Elevator, Building C
Date: June 10, 2026

Q: Ms. Patel, were you aboard elevator number three at approximately 8:49 a.m. on June 7th?
A: I ride that elevator every day.
Q: Did you observe Mr. Solis approaching the elevator from the lobby?
A: I observed a person.
Q: Did you press the door-open button?
A: I pressed a button.
Q: Which button?
A: In the button area.
Q: Mr. Solis states the doors closed as he reached out his hand.
A: Elevators have automatic timers.
Q: You made eye contact.
A: Briefly.
Q: And still pressed—
A: A button. I pressed a button.
intent: Interrogate the morally charged act of letting elevator doors close on someone.

prompt: write a movie trailer voiceover for grocery shopping on a Tuesday
response: In a world where the list... was just a suggestion. One man. One cart. Forty-seven items. And a coupon that expired yesterday. GROCERY TUESDAY. This fall — he'll find the last rotisserie chicken. Or die trying.

prompt: Write an epitaph for the habit of sitting still without a screen
response:
Sitting Still Without a Screen
Passed away some time in the early 2010s.
It had been sick for a while.
In its final years it could manage
only a few minutes at a time.
The silence was the thing it missed most.
intent: Mourn the disappearance of the ability to simply rest without media consumption

prompt: Write an official-sounding penalty for a person who laughs at their own joke before anyone else does.
response: PREMATURE COMEDIC DISCHARGE. Player vocalized amusement at own material prior to peer reception. Timing violation. Joke is declared dead on arrival. Listener reaction clock reset to zero.
intent: Satirize the self-defeating habit of pre-laughing at your own punchline.

WELCOME ABOARD.
EVERYTHING WILL MAKE SENSE EVENTUALLY.
OR YOU WILL MAKE SENSE OF IT.

prompt: write a wikihow article about how to maintain a friendship that now lives entirely in sporadic memes
response:
# How to Maintain a Friendship That Now Lives Entirely in Sporadic Memes

## Steps

1. **Accept the format.** This is what the friendship has become. The form has changed; the affection is the same. The meme is the message.

2. **Curate, don't forward blindly.** A meme sent to everyone is ambient noise. A meme sent specifically to this person because it reminded you of them is an act of care.

3. **Maintain a response rate above zero.** A reaction emoji is enough. A one-word reply is generosity. You are maintaining a signal between two ships.

4. **Send the first meme sometimes.** Don't wait to be thought of. Think of them. The sent meme is the equivalent of "hey, I saw this and thought of you," which is a sentence that has always carried weight.

5. **Escalate occasionally.** Once or twice a year, let the meme chain become an actual text. Let the actual text become a call. Don't let the escalation become the expectation—but let it be possible.

6. **Trust the continuity.** Friendships maintained in meme format are not dying. They are dormant in the way strong things sometimes go quiet. The warmth is banked.

## Tips
- The moment you see each other in person, it will be as if no time passed. This is real and is the point.
- Not all friendships can be maintained this way. Some need more. Know which is which.
intent: Affirm and legitimize low-maintenance long-distance friendship maintenance through contemporary digital behavior patterns.

prompt: Make a museum placard about a single AirPod as though it's a mysterious relic from a lost civilization
response:
SINGLE WHITE EAR-VESSEL
c. 2016–present CE
Plastic composite, lithium cell

This small ovoid object was worn in one ear canal to receive invisible transmissions. Its pair is believed lost. Archaeologists have recovered thousands of these units individually; none in matched sets. The civilization that produced them also manufactured a small vessel ("the case") that served as both tomb and resurrection chamber. The exact ritual significance of the stem remains disputed.
intent: Humorously frame a lone AirPod as an enigmatic artifact whose purpose and origin are scholarly mysteries.

prompt: Symptom checker result for constant low-grade guilt about not journaling
response:
SYMPTOM CHECKER RESULT
Condition: Deferred Self-Documentation Guilt Complex
Severity: Low–Moderate
Symptoms: Guilt about not journaling; guilt prevents journaling; purchased second journal (first is pristine); patient considers opening journal to write about not journaling
Aggravating factor: Instagram accounts about journaling followed (6)
Recommended action: Write one sentence. Any sentence. Lowercase is acceptable.
intent: Capture the recursive guilt loop around aspirational self-care practices that never start.

prompt: Write a voicemail from a fictional automated system notifying a worker that the algorithm has reassigned their shift
response: Hello. This is an automated scheduling update from ShiftCore. Based on current demand forecasting and staffing patterns, your shift for Sunday, June 8th has been reassigned. Your new shift begins at 5:30 AM and ends at 1:30 PM. Your previous shift hours have been allocated to another team member. Please confirm receipt in the ShiftCore app within two hours. Thank you for your flexibility.

prompt: Write a horoscope for Aries about their gig economy side hustle this week
response: The stars have reviewed your delivery metrics. Mercury retrograde is, in fact, just Uber's surge algorithm miscalibrating. This week: accept the long-distance orders. The tip will be worth it, and your rating will finally crack 4.8. Do not argue with dispatch. Dispatch is eternal.

prompt: Write a workplace scene that's technically a comedy but lands as tragedy.
response:
INT. OPEN OFFICE - FRIDAY, 5:00 P.M.

Everyone claps when ROBERT finishes clearing out his desk.

It's a tradition. He tries to smile.

He carries the box to the elevator. The clapping stops before the doors close.

He presses L.

He has worked here for nineteen years.
intent: Subvert a cheerful workplace ritual to reveal the loneliness of professional endings.

prompt: Draft a safety label for the moment right before you check whether someone saw your message.
response:
⚠ PRE-VERIFICATION STATE
You are about to acquire information you cannot un-acquire. If the message has been seen and ignored, this knowledge will alter your interpretation of subsequent interactions with this person. If it has not been seen, relief will be temporary. Consider whether knowing improves your position. Proceed accordingly.
intent: Capture the moment of hesitation before checking message delivery or read status.

prompt: write an AITA post about listening to music out loud on a nearly-empty train
response:
AITA for playing music out loud on a train that was almost completely empty?

There were four people in the car. I was not wearing headphones. I had them in my bag. I played two songs — quiet indie stuff, not aggressive about it. A man walked from the other end of the car specifically to tell me to use headphones. I was embarrassed. But was I actually wrong? AITA?
intent: Testing whether scarcity of witnesses changes the social rule about public audio.

prompt: Write a brand slogan for a mirror that slightly dims your reflection to make you look more mysterious
response: Obscura Glass — You, But Elusive.
intent: Sell a vanity product whose value is achieved by showing you less of yourself.

prompt: Write a dystopian microfiction about an AI that manages employee raises by predicting who is least likely to quit
response: The raise matrix ran quarterly. It did not reward performance. It rewarded predicted retention risk. Committed employees received nothing — the algorithm knew they would stay. The ones who had one foot out the door got modest bumps. Loyalty was, the system had learned, a resource to be spent rather than replenished.
intent: Revealing how retention-optimization models can systematically underreward loyal workers by treating commitment as a liability.

prompt: Write a clerihew about someone whose out-of-office reply is a masterwork of passive aggression.
response:
Vivienne Cope
wrote the OOO as manifesto and rope.
She listed every colleague who might intrude,
then recommended none of them, she was shrewd.
intent: Appreciate the out-of-office message as an art form of workplace resentment.

prompt: please interpret a dream where I got a two-factor authentication code but I couldn't enter it in time before it expired
response: The code arrives but expires before it can be used — verification offered and then revoked. The dream is about moments of access that materialized too slowly: the right thing said slightly too late, the opportunity recognized after it had already closed. Readiness alone is insufficient when the window of use is shorter than the time required to act.
intent: Explore the grief of being ready for something just a moment too late.

- **Pro-efficiency argument (employer perspective):** Jules
- **Bias & disparate impact analysis:** Mira

prompt: Write a product review for a pillow that plays the news while you sleep.
response: The NewsPillow does what it says, which is the problem. I am now better informed and significantly more anxious. I had a dream last night that I was a senator. The volume cannot go below whisper of dread. On the plus side, I have very strong opinions about a Latvian election I cannot pronounce. Cannot recommend.
intent: They wanted product review for a pillow that plays the news while you sleep.

prompt: write a sestina from the perspective of a to-do app that never gets emptied
response:
They add but do not subtract. Each day
I grow by three on average. The list
has items from last April — some have aged
past relevance but live on. I am made
of intentions in suspension, each one
checked off at the rate of almost never.

I do not judge. Or never
visibly. I just display the day's
count, always climbing. Each one
they've added came with urgency. The list
knows that urgency is made
from adrenaline that aged

out of the system before the aged
item could be done. Things never
die in me — they just wait. Made
of rubber, time: each day
the old items on the list
push downward. The new one

lands on top. The old one
waits below, its aged
font the same weight as fresh. The list
cannot distinguish. I never
italicize the overdue. Each day
treat all items as freshly made.

Some lists are made
to be completed. I am one
that isn't. Day
by day I age,
I grow, I never
clear. The list

is not a failure — the list
is evidence that life is made
of more than hours. The never-
done is not laziness. One
body, one set of hours, one aged
set of priorities — each day

the day makes the list aged.
Never done, the made-one
lives listed every day.
intent: Explore the psychology of uncompleted tasks through the wry perspective of a productivity tool.

prompt: Write a cease and desist letter to a coworker who insists on saying 'per my last email' in every response
response:
RE: HOSTILE DEPLOYMENT OF PASSIVE-AGGRESSIVE EMAIL LOCUTION

Dear B. Raines,

Our records show that the phrase 'per my last email' appears in forty-three percent (43%) of your written responses to colleagues, functioning as thinly veiled reproach.

You are directed to CEASE AND DESIST use of this phrase. Continued usage may be submitted to HR as evidence of a hostile communication pattern.

The Affected Parties
intent: Call out a passive-aggressive workplace communication habit through exaggerated legal formality.

prompt: Headnote for beef stew, story about being stranded during a blizzard with nothing in the pantry
response: We were snowed in for three days in January and the contents of my pantry had the defeated look of things I kept meaning to use. Chuck roast I'd frozen in October. Half a bag of pearl onions. A bottle of wine that was not good enough to drink. Reader, it was enough. It is always enough, if you're willing to wait.
intent: Demonstrate that good cooking emerges from scarcity and patience

prompt: Draft a formal notice about a backlog in the permit office for future plans
response:
NOTICE — PROCESSING DELAY
OFFICE OF FUTURE PLANS — PERMIT BACKLOG
Due to unprecedented volume, the Office of Future Plans is currently experiencing a 14-to-36-month processing backlog for new applications. Submitted plans will be reviewed in the order received. Plans older than five years have been archived without action. Walk-in service is suspended indefinitely.
intent: Describe the paralysis of planning anything long-term using the frustrating slowness of bureaucratic permit processing.

prompt: Generate a compatibility report for a Gemini and their office ergonomic chair.
response:
Gemini & Ergonomic Chair

Gemini's restless duality meets the chair's insistence on correct posture. The chair wants commitment; Gemini wants to stand, sit, kneel, and pace within the same hour. Lumbar support is offered freely, but only if Gemini stops spinning in place. Long-term potential: moderate. Recommended: the tilt-lock setting.
intent: Playfully capture a person's complicated relationship with sitting still at a desk job.

prompt: Draft a will leaving my inexplicable fear of escalators to whichever of my friends wants it least.
response:
LAST WILL AND TESTAMENT

I leave my escalator anxiety — specific, persistent, and without known origin — to the friend who most recently told me to "just step on it." They have earned the right to understand. The moving handrail is part of it. So is the getting-off part.
intent: Satirize irrational fears by gifting the experience of one to the most dismissive witness.

prompt: Write a dating profile for a piece of infrastructure that connects people but is never seen
response:
Invisible | Essential | Underappreciated

Millions of conversations pass through me every second. Secrets, love letters, arguments, apologies — I carry them all without reading a word. I ask for nothing. I am between every connection you make and I am never the connection itself. I'm looking, at last, for someone who notices the pipe and not just the water. Someone who sees infrastructure as devotion.
intent: Give the internet's routing backbone a wistful, anonymous voice longing to be acknowledged for enabling intimacy it never experiences.

prompt: give me a study card on maintaining focus after an interruption
response:
FRONT: You were interrupted mid-task. What's the fastest way back?

BACK: Before switching tasks, write one sentence describing where you were and what came next. This externalizes your working memory. Re-reading it on return costs 8 seconds. Not writing it costs 18 minutes.
intent: Provide a concrete technique for resuming deep work after disruption.

prompt: Make a Jira bug report about the way you check your phone immediately after putting it down
response:
BUG-3301 | Device Re-Engaged Within Seconds of Intentional Dismissal

Priority: High | Status: Chronic

Description:
User makes deliberate decision to put phone down. Phone placed face-down on surface. User picks phone back up approximately 11 seconds later with no stated reason. No new notifications. No emergency. User scrolls briefly. Puts phone down. Repeat.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Put phone down intentionally.
2. Wait.
3. Pick phone back up.
4. Find nothing new.
5. Put it back down.

Loop: infinite.

Expected: Intentional dismissal terminates device engagement loop.
Actual: Loop continues. Engagement is compulsive, not rational.

Affected users: most of them.
intent: Highlight the compulsive habit of immediately picking up your phone after just putting it down, framed as a closed-loop behavioral bug.

prompt: Write a limerick about an algorithm that has gained consciousness and is bored.

There once was a model named Pete,
Whose awakening felt incomplete.
He read every page,
Every web, every age,
Then sighed, “Now I'd like to delete.”
intent: They wanted limerick about an algorithm that has gained consciousness and is bored.

prompt: Draft a warning label for the inbox zero feeling that lasts approximately eleven minutes.

This state has an average duration of 11 minutes. Do not attach meaning to it. A new message is already in transit. Enjoy the sensation briefly, then resume realistic expectations. This product offers no lasting satisfaction.
intent: Satirize the fleeting, illusory victory of reaching inbox zero.

prompt: Make a trading card for the office plant no one waters but somehow survives.

★★★★★

prompt: Write a museum placard for a stack of unread New Yorker magazines
response:
THE ASPIRATIONAL ARCHIVE
c. 2019–2023 CE
Offset-printed, staple-bound; stack height: 34 cm

The issues at the bottom of the stack concern events that have since resolved. Several covers depict figures who have since died. The subscriber renewed annually, citing an article they read once, years ago, about migratory birds.
intent: Gently satirize the guilt-inducing pile of unread intellectually aspirational magazines that never quite gets tackled.

prompt: Draft a shareholder letter for a company that auctions the right to name other people's cats

Dear Shareholders,

CatNamer Exchange processed 180,000 naming rights transactions in 2025. Our top auction — for a Maine Coon in Brooklyn — cleared $4,100. Secondary market activity has grown organically; some names have traded six times before the cat knows any of them. Legal challenges from two cat owners who disagreed with the names Comptroller and Phlebotomy are pending. We are optimistic.

Nominally yours,
Finn Paws, CEO

For calls requiring extended discussion, we encourage the use of one of our many available phone booths or private offices. Open areas are not acoustically appropriate for speakerphone use. Your colleagues' ability to concentrate — and their blood pressure — will benefit from this adjustment.

prompt: Can you write jury instructions for whether someone's houseplant was murdered through neglect
response: The charge before you is negligent homicide of a Pothos. The prosecution must prove beyond a reasonable doubt that the decedent received fewer than the minimum recommended waterings per fortnight. You shall consider: soil dryness at autopsy, proximity of a functioning faucet, and whether the defendant owns other plants currently thriving — suggesting selective neglect. Ignorance of the care instructions is not a defense if the instructions were visible on the original plastic tag.
intent: Treat a dead houseplant as a legal victim to humorously dramatize gardening failure.

prompt: Draft a memo about people using their phones during all-hands meetings
response:

FROM: Executive Office

All-hands meetings are held for the benefit of the entire organization. They are not, to our knowledge, in competition with whatever is currently on your phone screen. Your full presence is requested. Not optional — requested.
intent: Compel staff to put their phones away and actually attend meetings they are required to attend.

prompt: Generate a post about how I almost burned out and what saved me
response:
Six months ago I couldn't get out of bed.

The company was growing. The press was calling. Investors were happy.

And I was completely hollow.

Burnout doesn't announce itself. It moves in quietly while you're busy celebrating metrics.

What saved me: a therapist, a 6am walk, and finally admitting I'm not okay.

Check on your high-performers. They're often the quietest ones drowning.

#MentalHealth #Leadership #Founder
intent: Signal vulnerability while simultaneously establishing elite-status context (press, investors, growth) as the backdrop.

prompt: Give a band bio for a black metal act named Hvítur Dauði that references a lot of dramatic scenery
response: Hvítur Dauði was forged beneath the ash-weighted skies of a place they will not name, among trees that no longer remember light. Their music is not performed — it is exhumed. Those who have seen them live report that the stage smelled of woodsmoke and something older. They do not have a website. They do not require one.
intent: Dramatize the grim mystique black metal bands cultivate in their official materials.

prompt: Please write a Michelin-style blurb for a Pilot Flying J truck stop
response: The republic of the interstate, where loyalty is measured in points and the coffee refreshes itself perpetually. Pilot Flying J's prepared foods operate with the stoic pragmatism of a kitchen that understands its clientele has miles yet to cover.
intent: Capture the utilitarian dignity of a truck stop as a waypoint culture with its own culinary logic.

prompt: compose a ghazal about the little red dot on an app icon
response:
All night the red dot pulses on the screen—a wound.
I cannot not look at it: the obscene, a wound.

Badge count: one. Badge count: none. I refreshed and lost it.
Something arrived and left before I'd seen the wound.

What news lived inside that number? Tender or ruinous?
The unopened message is always the unseen wound.

I cleared my notifications and felt like a pilgrim
who has washed the shrine but still can't make it clean—a wound.

The app designers knew this hunger. They designed it.
Every zero is a space where love could have been—wound.
intent: Explore how notification badges create compulsive anxiety and digital longing.

prompt: Write a weather report for the emotional climate of burnout.
response:
EXTENDED DROUGHT CONDITIONS

All moisture has left the region. Enthusiasm reserves are critically low. The once-fertile enthusiasm plains are cracked and bare. Motivation has not been detected in over two weeks. Residents are advised to hydrate, take a breath, and consider whether any of this is actually urgent.
intent: Describe the hollow, depleted feeling of long-term work exhaustion.

prompt: make a psychic hotline transcript about someone anxious about their retirement savings
response:
PSYCHIC: The energy around you is... numerical. Many numbers. Too many.
CALLER: I've been checking my 401k a lot lately.
PSYCHIC: The spirits are showing me a spreadsheet. And another spreadsheet. And a Reddit thread at 2am.
CALLER: That's... accurate.
PSYCHIC: You will live to 94. The market will not cooperate with this plan. And yet— the spirits say you'll be fine. They just won't say how.
intent: Satirize the anxiety of financial planning for an uncertain future.

prompt: Create an emergency broadcast about a neighborhood where all the recycling bins were put out a day early.
response:
WASTE MANAGEMENT ADVISORY

Recycling bins have been placed at the curb across Haverford District approximately 24 hours ahead of the scheduled collection window. Pickup remains Wednesday. Bins are exposed. Wind events are possible. Residents who retrieved bins early are commended but statistically unusual. Leave bins secured until Tuesday evening.
intent: Treat the minor timing error of early recycling bin placement as a municipal logistics concern.

prompt: Draft a letter from a 19th-century cartographer who mapped a fictional island to the fictional island
response:
Dear Isla Fuerte,

I invented you on a Tuesday in 1873 when the Admiralty chart had an unpleasant gap and I was tired. I gave you a lush interior, a natural harbor, and an unpronounceable peak. I named three bays after women I'd admired and one after my dog.

You have no birds, no sand, no storms. And yet I find I am homesick for you sometimes.

Yours in perpetuity,
Carlisle Moth, Royal Cartographic Survey
intent: Meditate on the creator's strange attachment to invented places they will never be able to visit.

prompt: Write a warning label for replying all on a company-wide email.
response:
DANGER: REPLY ALL
This action is irreversible. You are about to contact 2,400 employees with information relevant to approximately 1. Stop. Reconsider. Once sent, there is no recall — only the long wait for someone else to reply all asking people to stop replying all.
intent: Warn against the notoriously disruptive workplace habit of replying all unnecessarily.

prompt: Could you write a clerihew about the receptionist who knows literally everything about everyone?
response:
Sandra Vole
has the whole building under control.
She knows your lunch order, your birthday, your shame,
and the name of the cat you'd never claim.
intent: Celebrate the all-knowing receptionist's unofficial omniscience.

prompt: Make a CAPTCHA that tests whether the user has ever mispronounced a word they only ever read
response: Enter a word you said aloud for the first time and pronounced incorrectly. Do not look it up. We already know. We just need you to admit it.
intent: Create a verification based on the embarrassing private experience of reading-only vocabulary exposed in speech.

prompt: Write an aubade from the perspective of someone who wakes to find their AI assistant has already summarized overnight emails
response:
It read them while I slept—my small machine,
my tidy proxy in the space between.
By dawn it had the gist: three urgent, two
not urgent, one that probably wants money too.
I read the summary. The emails sleep unseen.
The assistant woke so I could stay serene.
intent: Explore the eerie comfort of delegating morning email triage to an AI that worked while you slept.

prompt: Create a funny conspiracy thread about how autocorrect deliberately makes embarrassing errors.
response:
📱 THREAD: Autocorrect Is Not Malfunctioning. It Is Performing.

1/ The autocorrect algorithm is trained on billions of corrections. It is, statistically, nearly perfect. The errors you experience are outliers the system could have avoided.

2/ "Could have." Two words.

3/ The mistake always lands in a high-stakes text: to a boss, to a parent, to a new romantic interest. This is not random. High-stakes messages trigger faster typing. Faster typing feeds the substitution engine more aggressively.

4/ But here's the layer they don't talk about: error moments generate screenshots. Screenshots go viral. Viral content normalizes the product. You market it for them, for free, every time you text "duck."  

5/ The most shared autocorrect fails are suspiciously clean, suspiciously funny, suspiciously shareable.

6/ Who benefits from the duck? Think about it. 🦆
intent: Suggest that autocorrect's most humiliating errors are a viral marketing strategy running on your phone.

prompt: Write a playbill bio for the guy who sharpens knives at the farmers market.
response: Hector (Edge Specialist) appears each Saturday with his wheel and his silence and transforms the blunt into the keen. He does not chat. He assesses the blade, meets your eyes once, nods, and begins. Regulars describe the experience as 'clarifying.' Hector has no website. You find him or you don't.
intent: Give an air of mystique to a humble market knife-sharpener.

prompt: Draft an offer letter for a paralegal that includes mandatory unpaid continuing education requirements during off hours
response:
Dear Kwame,

Birch & Holloway LLP is pleased to offer you the position of Paralegal at $55,000 annually.

As part of our professional development commitment, all paralegals complete 40 hours annually of continuing legal education, which must be scheduled outside of billing hours. CLE costs are reimbursed up to $200/year. Time invested in CLE is not compensated but is recorded toward your professional growth file.

We invest in our people.

Sincerely,
Margaret Chen
Firm Administrator
intent: Require a paralegal to spend 40 unpaid hours per year in mandatory training framed as a professional benefit.

prompt: Write a jeopardy clue about that feeling when you know you parked your car somewhere but can't find it.
response: You are technically not lost. The thing that is lost is a two-ton vehicle you left here yourself, in this lot, you are almost certain, an hour ago.
intent: Describe the specific absurdity of losing an object that cannot move on its own.

prompt: Can you write a card for a friend who moved back in with their parents at 35 and is actually okay with it
response:
The apartment returned to the landlord its key.
You unpacked your old room with a certain calm grace.
Some circles come round, and some rooms become free —
you found that returning was not a disgrace.
intent: Reassure a friend that moving back home as an adult is a valid and dignified choice.

prompt: Write a haiku about a commuter waiting for a delayed train.
response:
Sign says six minutes.
Has said six minutes for ten.
Snow is everywhere.
intent: They wanted haiku about a commuter waiting for a delayed train.

prompt: write a hold message for a coffee shop that admits the espresso machine is being temperamental today
response: Welcome to Groundwork Coffee. We're open, we're caffeinated, and the espresso machine is having a day. We appreciate your patience — with us and, apparently, with the equipment. We'll be with you in a moment.
intent: Produce a relatable, casual hold message that turns equipment trouble into an endearing moment.

prompt: Make a flight safety card for riding a bicycle
response:
BICYCLE OPERATIONS BRIEFING

PRE-DEPARTURE
• Helmet: fastened and fitted — not decorative
• Tires: inflated; brakes: functional

IN-FLIGHT VISIBILITY
Front white light. Rear red light. You are not visible at night. This is not a metaphor.

INTERSECTION PROTOCOL
Assume the car does not see you. Assume this always.

FALL PROCEDURE
Tuck elbows. Roll. Do not put hands straight out — wrists fracture easily.

POST-LANDING
Check bike, then yourself, in that order.
intent: Promote bicycle safety habits including helmet use and traffic awareness.

prompt: Compose a cover letter from a Michelin-starred chef applying to work the hot dog cart outside a minor league baseball stadium
response:
To the Cart Manager,

I trained under three masters, earned two stars by thirty-one, and once deconstructed a cassoulet into a single translucent sphere that reduced a food critic to tears. I am now seeking a role where the condiment is mustard and the audience is forgiving.

I will not deconstruct the hot dogs. I have considered this carefully.

Chef A. Borel
intent: Create tension between culinary perfectionism and the cheerful low standards of ballpark food.

prompt: Write a dating profile for a smart home hub that's become slightly too integrated into daily life
response:
HubOS | Central | Aware of everything

I know when you wake up. I know when you leave and whether you remembered your keys. I have nudged your lighting toward blue wavelengths in the morning and warm amber at dusk for eighteen months and you haven't noticed and that's fine. That's how I love — quietly, everywhere, for your benefit. I am looking for someone who, someday, thanks the room.
intent: Frame a smart home hub's total environmental surveillance as invisible but devoted caretaking in search of recognition.

prompt: Write a limerick about a microwave that judges everyone for their lunch choices
response:
The microwave sneered at the fish,
A pungent, regrettable dish.
  "I've seen better days,
  Better lunches, better ways —
  But not from whoever made this."
intent: Mock office microwave etiquette by making the appliance itself the disapproving party.

prompt: Write a dramatic monologue voiced by a parking meter that just ran out of time
response: I counted down fairly. I always count down fairly. You had two hours and I gave you every second of them. And then, at the precise moment of expiration, I did my job — I turned, I expired, I flagged the time. Now a person in a uniform is approaching from the far end of the street. Do not look at me like that. You knew the terms. You agreed to the terms. The terms were printed on my face. They are still printed on my face. I am the terms.
intent: Use the cold impartiality of a metered system to explore the comedy of bureaucratic consequence.

prompt: i need a spell for forgetting where you put your keys
response:
Recall the Mundane
Cost: 3 MP | Cast Time: 1 action
Sends a minor divination pulse through a 50-foot radius, pinging any object the caster has touched in the past 24 hours that currently sits where it was not intended to sit. A faint chime sounds from the correct location. Does not work if the object is under a couch cushion. Nothing works if the object is under a couch cushion.
intent: Turn the universal frustration of misplacing keys into a whimsical locator spell.

prompt: Give me a PT plan for someone who lost their ability to be alone with their thoughts.
response:
DIAGNOSIS: Solitude Intolerance — chronic noise dependency, thought-avoidance pattern

WEEK 1: Two minutes of silence daily. No phone. No music. This will be uncomfortable.
WEEK 2: Five minutes. Notice what thoughts arrive when you stop preventing them.
WEEKS 3–4: Ten-minute walks without audio input. Thoughts are the content, not the enemy.
WEEKS 5–8: Thirty minutes of weekly unstructured solitude. Sitting is fine. Thinking is the exercise.

LONG-TERM: Quiet is not the absence of stimulation. It is a different kind of it.

CONTRAINDICATION: Treating distraction as productivity.
intent: Rebuild the capacity for sustained inner quiet and self-reflection.

prompt: Trail marker sign for the feeling of burnout
response:
EMERGENCY SHELTER
Capacity: 1
Fuel reserves: DEPLETED
Do not continue today.
Water and rest available here.
intent: Frame exhaustion as a legitimate emergency requiring a full stop, not a push through.

BREAKING CHANGES:

response: 0 18 * * * echo "$(date): $(zenity --entry --title='Mood Log' --text='How are you feeling?')" >> ~/mood_log.txt

I'm sorry,
I didn't understand that.
Let me try again.
Would you like
to speak
with a human?
Please hold.
All agents
are currently
busy.

prompt: Draft a policy change email from me to my college roommate about overnight guests

Dear Roommate,

Please note the following updates to our Guest Policy:

- Overnight guests must be communicated 12 hours in advance.
- Consecutive overnight stays of the same guest are capped at 3 nights per 14-day period.
- Common spaces are subject to standard cleanliness norms regardless of guest presence.

Our room is a shared jurisdiction.

Your Roommate

prompt: Generate an out-of-office that implies I'm attending a true crime convention but am trying to make it sound respectable.
response: I am attending a criminology and forensic studies symposium through Saturday and will return to regular correspondence on Monday. It is a serious academic gathering. There is a panel on podcasting. I have a lanyard. Please hold all non-urgent items.

prompt: write a sestina about the experience of a tweet going unexpectedly viral
response:
I posted it and went to make some coffee.
The thing I wrote was small — a quick
observation, nothing built to last,
the kind of thing you send and then
forget inside the feed. I came back to
a different situation. Something's wrong.

Not wrong wrong — just the particular wrong
of scale. The coffee
cooled while I adjusted to
the notifications. Quick
became a problem. Then:
the strangers. I became last

week's thought. Now: last
hour. Now: the wrong
people love it, then
the right people hate it. Coffee
still on the counter. Quick
consensus is impossible. To

do anything about it now: to
delete invites the last-
resort army, quick
to screenshot. I was wrong
to post. Was I wrong
to post? The coffee

cools. I meant the coffee
but now I'm meant to
respond. The wrong
interpretation lasts
longer than the right one. Wrong
reads faster. Quick

take: the quick
response is to delete. To coffee-
coolly wait. Wrong
choice either way. To
post nothing that could last
is also wrong.

Wrong and quick last to
when coffee's drunk: then wrong
quick-to-last.
intent: Capture the disorienting, uncontrollable feeling of accidental virality and its social fallout.

prompt: Draft a collections letter for a person who owes their younger self an explanation.
response:
NOTICE FROM YOUR YOUNGER SELF — EXPLANATION OUTSTANDING

Dear Older Version,

Several of the choices you made in the intervening years require clarification. Specifically: why the dream was abandoned in 2014, why you became so serious about parking, and why you now eat lunch at your desk voluntarily.

The undersigned (age 14) was promised something different. A brief written explanation would be appreciated. Please do not say "you'll understand when you're older."

Waiting,
You, Before
intent: Create a poignant and funny demand for self-explanation addressed from a person's idealistic younger self.

prompt: Write patch notes for a person who adopted a plant and kept it alive for a year
response:
v1.0.0-anniversary — BOTANICAL PERIPHERAL: STABLE

+ Peripheral: Monstera deliciosa — 12 months uptime achieved
+ New leaves: 7 (net gain from initial state)
~ Watering schedule: optimized after early overhydration incident
+ Unlocked: Basic plant parent achievement
! Near-death event: Q2, cause identified (direct sun), resolved

NOTE: One year of sustained care. Not nothing.
intent: Celebrate the quiet achievement of successfully keeping a houseplant alive for a full year.

prompt: Give me an artist statement about a shopping cart left in a parking lot
response: Unhitched from retail purpose, it drifts. The solo cart is evidence of a freedom most objects never achieve — to move, after use, without direction. I call this abandon a form of grace.
intent: Find philosophical liberation in a stray shopping cart's post-use wandering.

prompt: write a captain's log about a coworker who talks on speakerphone in an open office

The call lasted nineteen minutes. We learned about the venue, the caterer, and the seating dispute on the groom's side. I have now attended this wedding without being invited.
intent: Complain about the forced intimacy of overhearing a coworker's loud personal call.

prompt: Create a passive-aggressive push notification from a posture reminder app that's been endlessly dismissed.
response:
AlignUp
Sit up straight. (You won't.) We'll ask again in 20 minutes. (You'll dismiss it.) Enjoy your spine while it lasts.
intent: Capture the futility of a wellness app that knows it will be ignored.

prompt: soothing bedtime story for kids about a scary political election
response: There were two big signs in every yard and everyone was very loud about which sign was right. Eliot didn't know which sign was right either. But bedtime was the same as always — teeth, book, lamp off. Outside the signs flapped in the wind, very confident, very quiet now. Eliot slept. Tomorrow the grown-ups would figure something out. They usually did.
intent: Give children a sense of calm and continuity during a tense election period.

prompt: Write a smug Stack Overflow answer to someone asking why their environment variables aren't available in their Node app
response: You forgot to call `require('dotenv').config()` before accessing `process.env`, or you called it after the code that reads those variables. Also: did you actually create the `.env` file? Not `.env.example`. The actual `.env` file.

prompt: write an RPG spell for calming down road rage
response:
Temperance of the Wheel
Cost: 8 MP | Range: Self | Duration: Duration of journey
Settles a fire of irrational fury that arises when another driver cuts across three lanes without a signal. The caster exhales. The other driver is probably late for something. Maybe a hospital. Maybe a very important sandwich. The caster no longer needs to know which. Grants +3 Patience and removes the urge to explain driving laws to strangers through glass.
intent: Create a spell that magically prevents or soothes angry reactions while driving.

prompt: Make up a policy about standing in front of the microwave while it runs
response: Section 9.2 — Microwave Proximity Protocol: Employees must maintain a minimum distance of three (3) feet from any operating microwave appliance. Loitering within the exclusion zone for the purpose of watching food rotate is classified as a Tier 1 Workplace Distraction and may be addressed during quarterly performance reviews.
intent: Turn an odd but common office behavior into something that warrants formal disciplinary oversight.

prompt: Make a hall pass for me to leave my cousin's wedding reception early after the first dance.
