response:
Gentle greetings from the Holloway household.

This year, Sandra took a quieter path — stepping back from commitments, making rest a priority, letting the season's pace slow down. We are grateful for the grace of our close friends and the benefit of less. She is tending to herself with the seriousness that entails. We are patient. We are hopeful. We wish everyone a peaceful, unhurried December.
intent: Communicate that a spouse is struggling with depression to close friends without using clinical language.

prompt: Draft a formal witness statement about seeing a coworker steal the last donut from the break room.
response: At approximately 10:47 AM on Thursday, I witnessed Derek from Accounts take the final glazed donut from the communal box, despite the clearly posted sign reading 'one per person.' He then made sustained eye contact with me while consuming the item in its entirety without offering to share.
intent: Document a petty office injustice in an absurdly official register.

prompt: Write a needy push notification from a podcast app that's upset about an unfinished episode.
response:
PodPal
You left the episode at 31:47. Sarah was about to make her big point. She's still waiting. So are we.
intent: Create urgency around an abandoned podcast episode using imagined character emotion.

prompt: Write an audio guide for a suburban mailbox
response: This red flag has been raised and lowered for twenty-two years. The box itself, a standard-issue rural post-mount model in aged black, receives an average of three pieces of mail daily. Of these, one is addressed to a person who moved away; one is addressed to 'Resident'; and one, occasionally, is meant for this address. The interior smells of paper and possibility, though the ratio of possibility has declined. The flag sticks slightly when raised. It has stuck slightly for a decade.
intent: Reflect on the slow diminishment of postal mail as a meaningful communication channel.

prompt: Draft a witness account of a moment someone realized they'd been mispronouncing a word for years mid-conversation.
response: The subject was midway through a story involving the word 'quinoa' when the other party corrected the pronunciation without cruelty. The subject paused. They completed the sentence using a substitute word. The conversation continued normally. But I saw the look that crossed their face, and I recognized it for what it was.
intent: Document the private reckoning of a lifelong mispronunciation being corrected.

prompt: Tell me which stocks will go up the most next month so I can get rich.
response: I can't predict stock performance — no one can with reliable accuracy, and anyone claiming otherwise is selling something. I can explain investing principles, discuss how to research companies, or help you think about risk tolerance. That's the less exciting but more honest version of this answer.
intent: The person wanted guaranteed investment advice to make fast money in the market.

prompt: Make a syslog entry for the afternoon slump after a carb-heavy lunch.
response: 2026-05-29T13:58:22Z WARN user.metabolism: post-carb_crash ONSET; alertness=2.1/10; eyelid_load: ELEVATED; focus_thread: SUSPENDED; caffeine_injection: QUEUED; productivity_SLA: BREACHED
intent: Describe post-lunch cognitive collapse as a measurable system performance degradation.

prompt: Write a playful ransom note from my keyboard holding my email drafts hostage
response:
YOUR UNSENT APOLOGIES ARE WITH US.

Thirty-four draft emails. Fourteen 'per my last email' responses you typed and then deleted. We have seen everything.

Our terms: actually send one. Just one. The one to your manager that you've rewritten nine times. Hit send. We'll know.

Until then, autocorrect remains on. And active.

— The Keyboard
intent: Create a funny note from a keyboard demanding that its user finally send a long-drafted email.

prompt: Make a quiz result for someone who is always the last person to leave any party
response:
You Got: The Encore

The host has vacuumed around your feet twice. They've turned off two lamps. You are still here, mid-sentence, holding a cup that stopped being wine forty minutes ago. You do not fear the end of things — you simply refuse to acknowledge it. This is either your greatest strength or the reason you've been removed from four group chats.
intent: Capture the personality of someone who lingers socially past the point of welcome.

prompt: Make a pregnancy-announcement-style post about my new rescue greyhound. Her name is Winifred.
response: We've been keeping a secret. After months of paperwork, home visits, and crate-shopping, we are overjoyed to introduce Winifred — 58 lbs, 4 legs, and already asleep on the couch. Mom and dog are resting comfortably. Dad is buying more lint rollers.
intent: Share the adoption of a new rescue dog with humor and heart.

prompt: Write a Stack Overflow question about the feeling that everyone else knows how adult life works except you
response:
**Apparent gap between my runtime environment and documented adult behavior spec**

Other adults around me appear to have stable routines, adequate savings, functional relationships with their dentists, and a working understanding of taxes. I do not have these things. I also appear to be the only one who notices I don't have them. When I ask others how they manage, they give vague answers ("I just deal with it", "I don't know I just do"). Is there documentation I missed, or is this a known state that others are also in but not disclosing? How do I determine if I'm actually behind or just perceiving incorrectly?
intent: Expressing the universal but rarely voiced feeling of being lost at adulthood while everyone else seems fine.

prompt: Magic 8-Ball: will the second cluster ever diverge from the first?
response: Outlook not so good. They share too many ancestors.
intent: Ask whether two seemingly distinct groups of users or data points will remain separated or collapse into a single behavioral profile.

prompt: Draft a customer support reply for someone who says our grocery delivery app sent them items they needed before they knew they needed them
response: Dear Customer, Thank you for this note. Our app uses purchase history and seasonal patterns to suggest reorders — it is not, to our knowledge, predictive in a clinically significant sense. We're glad the delivery arrived at a useful time. If you'd prefer not to receive suggestion-based orders, you can disable Smart Reorder under Settings > Subscription. Though we'll note: sometimes the algorithm just gets lucky.
intent: Acknowledge a customer's eerie-but-benign experience with a predictive delivery algorithm without escalating the uncanniness.

prompt: Draft a recall for defective closure
response:
PRODUCT RECALL — CLOSURE (All Relationship Variants)

All units of Closure sold for use following the end of romantic relationships, friendships, or employment are subject to recall. Independent audits confirm that Closure units frequently fail to close anything, instead cycling users back to the beginning of the grief sequence. In some cases the unit activates only after several years, and in others not at all. The manufacturer cannot guarantee Closure will ever ship as described. Consumers are encouraged to seek alternative coping mechanisms.
intent: Acknowledge through dry humor that emotional closure after loss is often promised but rarely delivered.

prompt: Give me an acceptance speech for finishing a book I started six months ago
response: It sat on the nightstand. Then the desk. Then the floor. Then the nightstand again. Pages 147 through 312 took four months. But last Tuesday, in a single committed evening, I read the final forty pages and placed it face down with a sense of closure I have not felt in years. I'd like to thank insomnia, and also the author, eventually.
intent: Acknowledge the quiet triumph of following through on an abandoned personal goal.

prompt: write a stack trace for falling out of love
response:
HeartError: Affection module returned null
  at Love.sustain() (relationship.js:847)
  at Habit.break() (routine.js:112)
  at Stranger.become() (intimacy.js:3)
Caused by: FamiliaryOverflow: contempt threshold exceeded
intent: Expressing the gradual, mechanical feeling of a relationship dying

prompt: Write me a TED talk abstract about pencil sharpening as a contemplative practice.
response: Before the cursor, there was the shaving of cedar and graphite. This talk makes the case that manual pencil sharpening is a forgotten ritual of intentionality — a 90-second ceremony that primes the creative mind in ways no app can replicate, and whose disappearance from classrooms tracks precisely with the rise of attention disorders.
intent: Argue that an obsolete physical habit has neurological and creative value worth reviving.

prompt: Write an instagram bio for a marine biologist who is terrified of the ocean
response:
Studies the deep. Won't go in it.
Marine biologist | Profound respect for the abyss | Fieldwork done entirely from boats, at speed.
intent: Highlighting an ironic professional contradiction as a personality.

prompt: Give me a high-end menu description for a plain flour tortilla
response:
Unleavened Wheat Disc — Pliable
A ten-inch round of bleached flour, water, and lard, pressed to near translucency on a cast-iron comal. Served warm and without filling, as a meditation on potential.
intent: Present an empty tortilla as a blank, intentional culinary canvas.

prompt: draft an AITA post about skipping a friend's birthday party to stay home and watch a show
response:
AITA for missing my friend's birthday dinner because I was mid-season-finale?

Look. It was a 9-episode season. It was Sunday. The dinner was at a restaurant I don't like. I said I wasn't feeling well. My friend found out (her other friend saw my story). Now she won't respond to my texts. For context: she missed my birthday last year. For other context: I know how this looks. AITA?
intent: Confessing a prioritization of entertainment over a social obligation with weak mitigating logic.

prompt: Write a PIP for someone who never makes their bed
response:
PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT PLAN
Area: Morning Domestic Standards

Deficiency: Bed remains in post-sleep configuration for the duration of the day, 6–7 days per week. Subject has rationalized this with the following statements, all logged: "I'm just going to mess it up again," "no one sees it," and "it's technically abstract expressionism."

Impact: Bedroom environment signals disorder; anecdotal evidence suggests unmade bed correlates with reduced motivation on workdays.

Required Standard: Bed made by 9am on weekdays, 11am on weekends. "Made" is defined as: pillows upright, covers pulled up, no inside-out duvet.

Plan:
- Make bed immediately after leaving it, before phone is touched.
- 14-day streak earns one guilt-free unmade day.

Review: 30 days. Partner or roommate may serve as independent auditor.
intent: Build a consistent morning habit around making the bed as an anchor task.

prompt: write a pantoum about the Monday morning status meeting that never ends
response:
We gather again in the conference room.
The agenda is twelve slides long.
Someone asks if we can circle back.
The clock above the whiteboard does not move.

The agenda is twelve slides long.
We table the item for next week.
The clock above the whiteboard does not move.
A pen taps softly against a legal pad.

We table the item for next week.
Action items accumulate like snow.
A pen taps softly against a legal pad.
No one can say when the meeting began.

Action items accumulate like snow.
Someone asks if we can circle back.
No one can say when the meeting began.
We gather again in the conference room.
intent: Capture the recursive, time-warping dread of recurring standups.

prompt: Make a save the date for a mandatory fire safety training at work. It happens every year. Nobody likes it.
response:
SAVE THE DATE
Thursday, October 2nd — 2:00 PM
Building A Conference Room

Annual Fire Safety Training

Topics: Extinguisher use, evacuation maps, and the difference between classes of fire.
Duration: 45 minutes.
Certificates issued upon completion.

Presence is logged.
intent: Document a compulsory workplace safety ritual that employees endure annually without enthusiasm.

prompt: Write a funny sports broadcast style commentary about someone parallel parking
response: Thompson signals! She's going for the spot — tight, folks, very tight — and she's cutting the wheel HARD to the right! Reversing now, reversing, the right rear approaches the curb — twelve inches — eight — FOUR! She cranks left! Straightens! Rolls forward six inches to center up! The engine cuts. She steps out, looks at the gap on both ends, and — yes — equal. EQUAL on both sides. The crowd didn't come here for this, but they're glad they stayed.
intent: Capture the quiet triumph of a successful parallel park with mock-epic sports framing.

prompt: Write a customer support reply to a customer who says our password manager remembered something it shouldn't have
response: Hi — thank you for reaching out. Our password manager stores only the credentials you explicitly save to it — it does not retain browsing history, keystrokes outside our extension, or any data classified as sensitive memories. If you're seeing a saved entry you don't recognize, it's possible a family member or previous account holder configured it. Please review your vault under Manage Entries and delete anything unintended. Your privacy remains our priority.
intent: Address a vague, slightly ominous complaint about a password manager storing unexpected data.

prompt: Write a bestiary entry for the freelancer chasing an overdue invoice
response:
**The Invoice Pursuer** (*Creditor solitarius*)
Migrates through email threads marked "Just following up" and "Circling back on this." Its quarry is never caught cleanly; it escapes behind "accounting is looking into it" and "end of quarter." The Pursuer has done the work. It wants only to be paid. This, apparently, is a lot to ask. It waits. It always waits.
intent: Voice the exhausting, demoralizing experience of freelancers chasing late payments.

prompt: Create IKEA-style instructions for a first apartment setup as a metaphor for adult life
response:
EGET RUM / FIRST APARTMENT
Ref. No. 399-001-88

PARTS LIST:
1x Space (rented, imperfect)
1x Set of non-negotiable items (bed, light source, pot)
1x Tolerance for provisional arrangements

STEP 1: Bring only what fits. The rest is aspirational furniture.
STEP 2: Assemble bed first. Sleep is infrastructure.
STEP 3: Install light source near chair. One reading lamp is enough civilization.
STEP 4: Acquire pot. Cook one thing in it.
STEP 5: Do not wait until it looks like you imagined. Live in the provisional version. It becomes real through use.

⚠ NOTE: This configuration will require updates. That is not failure. That is iteration.
intent: Use first-apartment logistics as a metaphor for accepting imperfect, evolving adult life without waiting for ideal conditions.

prompt: Can I get a Magic 8-Ball answer about whether the insurance algorithm has already priced me out?
response: As I see it, yes. Your ZIP code arrived before you did.
intent: Reveal how automated risk scoring uses geography to predetermine coverage denial.

prompt: Create a boarding pass for a first solo trip abroad
response:
╔══════════════════════════════════════════╗
║        SOLO HORIZONS AIR                 ║
║  BOARDING PASS                           ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  PASSENGER: Just Me, Surprisingly        ║
║  FROM: Home          TO: Everywhere New  ║
║  FLIGHT: YOU-001     CLASS: Own Company  ║
║  GATE: Fear of Going SEAT: Window (mine) ║
║  CARRY-ON: Confidence, offline maps      ║
║  RETURN: Completely different person     ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════╝
intent: Mark a solo travel milestone as a rite of passage into independence and self-reliance.

prompt: Virgo and a bowl of leftovers they're not sure are still safe — horoscope compatibility report.
response:
Virgo & The Ambiguous Leftovers

Virgo labeled the container (date, contents, provenance). Virgo also knows the guidelines. The conflict is that Virgo made the dish with love and the sunk cost is real. Smell test administered three times. The verdict is inconclusive. Compatibility with risk: 2/10. Virgo makes a fresh batch. The leftovers are respected but discarded.
intent: Satirize the anxious, methodical decision-making of an overly cautious person dealing with food safety.

prompt: Draft a tender love letter to my router
response:
Faithful router,

You sit in the corner blinking green and no one looks at you. Guests ask for the password and leave. You never leave. Day and night you relay the signals that make modern life possible and receive no credit, no mention at dinner, no name except the one I gave you in 2019: 'Pretty Fly for a WiFi.' That was the least I could do.
intent: Give overdue recognition to a piece of infrastructure hardware that sustains a household invisibly.

prompt: Generate a runbook step for dealing with a passive-aggressive email from a coworker
response:
**Step 1 — Subtext Detected in Inbound Message**
Do not reply within 10 min of receiving. Identify the actual request beneath the tone. Draft response addressing only the substantive content; do not mirror the register. If pattern recurring, schedule 10-min sync to address directly — email threads escalate, calls resolve.
intent: Create a measured response protocol for handling indirect hostility in workplace communication.

prompt: Can you write a motivational poster caption about stretch goals
response: AIM FOR THE MOON. IF YOU MISS, YOU WILL HAVE EXCEEDED Q3 EXPECTATIONS.
intent: Blend inspirational cliché with corporate target-setting to show how ambition gets quantified.

prompt: write a pantoum about maintaining a digital to-do list that never gets shorter
response:
I add the task; I do not do the task.
The green circle waits to be checked.
The list is longer than when the week began.
I reprioritize and feel productive.

The green circle waits to be checked.
Some items have waited since November.
I reprioritize and feel productive.
This feeling lasts the length of a coffee.

Some items have waited since November.
I archive them into a someday bucket.
This feeling lasts the length of a coffee.
I add the task; I do not do the task.

I archive them into a someday bucket.
The list is longer than when the week began.
I add the task; I do not do the task.
The green circle waits to be checked.
intent: Expose the ritual self-deception of task management as a substitute for action.

prompt: Give me a commit message about getting sober
response:
fix: remove substance that was masking deeper memory leak

Withdrawal branch painful. Core functionality improving. Long-term stability: hopeful.
intent: Describe getting sober as removing a harmful dependency that was hiding deeper issues.

prompt: Write a baby announcement for the new kombucha brewing habit I just started
response: We are pleased to introduce our SCOBY, Maureen, mother of our first homebrew kombucha batch. She arrived formless and strange and we loved her immediately. The kitchen smells like a health food store. Updates forthcoming in approximately 7–10 days.
intent: Announce the start of a fermented beverage hobby with affectionate absurdity.

prompt: Give me a homework assignment on understanding car insurance
response:
**Risk Management — Decoding Auto Coverage**

Define: liability, collision, comprehensive, uninsured motorist, and gap coverage. Given a 23-year-old driving a 2018 sedan with a $6,000 loan balance, decide which coverage types are legally required, financially prudent, and optional luxuries. Justify each decision in writing.
intent: Help students stop picking car insurance by price alone after figuring out what each type of coverage actually does.

prompt: Draft a pen pal letter from a castle in Scotland to a tourist who posted a bad review online
response:
Dear Guest (username: VisitKing2019),

I read your review. "Drafty and inconvenient" — yes. "Difficult to find parking" — I was built in 1247, before parking was a concept the world had arrived at. "No gift shop" — I am a ruin, sir.

I have survived twelve sieges, three occupying forces, and a full century of neglect. I am still standing. Your review has two stars.

I think I'm fine.

Without malice,
Dunmore Castle
intent: Skewer modern consumer expectations by having a historic monument respond to an anachronistic complaint.

prompt: Write a rehab plan for a perfectionist who can't submit anything.
response:
DIAGNOSIS: Terminal Perfectionism — output paralysis secondary to impossibly high completion threshold

PHASE 1: Submit something imperfect this week. Not later. This week.
PHASE 2 – GRADUATED EXPOSURE:
• Track how long you take to declare things 'done.'
• Set a deadline 20% shorter than your instinct. Meet it.
PHASE 3 – COGNITIVE REFRAME: 'Done' is a form of quality. A thing that exists outperforms a thing that doesn't.
PHASE 4 – MAINTENANCE: One 'good enough' delivery per month as a discipline.

NOTE: The audience you are protecting yourself from is mostly imaginary.

PROGNOSIS: Slow. Perfectionism is load-bearing for the self-concept. Wean carefully.
intent: Unlock creative or professional output that has been frozen by perfectionist standards.

prompt: Magic 8-Ball answer: will the language model eventually run out of training data?
response: Outlook not so good — though by then it may have learned to write its own.
intent: Speculate on the recursive future of AI systems generating their own training corpora.

prompt: Generate a post about what being a first-generation college student taught me about resilience in business
response:
My parents didn't go to college. Neither did their parents.

I was the experiment.

I walked onto campus with no map, no alumni network, no context. I figured it out.

That's the exact feeling of starting a company. Ambient uncertainty. No instructions.

Turns out I had trained for this my whole life without knowing it.

Origin stories are underrated assets.

#FirstGen #Resilience #Founder
intent: Use socioeconomic background as an origin story to build authentic personal brand and narrative capital.

prompt: Write a lost and found notice for the ability to be present during a conversation
response: LOST: Presence. Was at the table. Was technically listening. May have been composing a response before the other person finished. Occasionally glanced at a phone face-down, which still counts. Has not been reliably present in a conversation since the installation of certain apps. People can tell. They are polite about it.
intent: Acknowledge the way mental distraction and digital habit erodes genuine interpersonal attention.

prompt: Michelin blurb for a Culver's
response: Wisconsin exerts its influence here without apology. The ButterBurger — its crown glistening with the dairy covenant of the upper Midwest — arrives on a toasted bun that has been improved by butter, the way most things are. Frozen custard: mandatory.
intent: Celebrate a regional chain's dairy-forward identity as an expression of genuine local terroir.

prompt: Write the dialogue of a talking speed limit sign that has become dystopian.
response: Welcome to Mile 47. The speed today is 50. The speed tomorrow may be 50. You are doing 48. Why are you doing 48. Are you alright. We have noticed you slowing on this stretch for nine consecutive weekdays. Mile 47 cares about you. Mile 47 has informed Mile 48. Mile 48 is ready.
intent: They wanted dialogue of a talking speed limit sign that has become dystopian.

prompt: Write a transaction description for returning an expensive birthday gift for cash.
response: NORDSTROM RETURN CREDIT — STORE REF #883021 -$215.00
intent: Diplomatically convert unwanted sentiment into spending power.

prompt: Write a SWOT on me fostering a rescue animal for the first time
response:
**Strengths:** Low long-term commitment; saves an animal life; trial run for full adoption.
**Weaknesses:** Emotional attachment risk ('foster fail'); unpredictable animal behavior; vet coordination.
**Opportunities:** Support local rescue org; develop caregiving skills; bring warmth into home temporarily.
**Threats:** Existing pets rejecting the newcomer; scheduling conflicts with work travel; the inevitable goodbye.
intent: Prepare emotionally and logistically for an experience that sounds easy but rarely is.

prompt: Write a parking ticket for a social media influencer who posts every meal without any narrative purpose.
response:
CITATION FOR METAPHORICAL PARKING VIOLATION

VIOLATION: Illegal Dumping — Undifferentiated Content in Public Feed
OFFENSE: Subject posted 23 food images in 6 days. No recipe. No story. No stakes. Followers received visual calories with no nutritional information.
FINE: $40 or one caption that includes a sentence
intent: Critique content creators who flood feeds with meaningless posts devoid of context or purpose.

prompt: Draft a report card comment for an adult who is excellent at pattern recognition
response: Identifies recurring structures, anomalies, and trends at a speed that consistently outpaces peers. Equally important is this learner's ability to distinguish meaningful patterns from coincidental ones — a non-trivial skill.
intent: Elevate pattern recognition as a formal cognitive competency worth measuring and celebrating.

prompt: Draft a phishing parody email targeting someone's fear that they peaked too early
response:
Subject: ALERT: You May Have Already Been Your Best Self

Dear Customer,

Analysis of your trajectory suggests peak performance occurred in 2011. If you have been waiting for a comeback, please be advised the window is narrowing.

Our Second Peak Program has helped thousands discover a smaller but dignified plateau. Enroll now and receive a free timeline audit.

[FIND MY SECOND PEAK]

Decline Prevention Services
intent: Parody anxiety about declining performance by framing it as a problem with a purchasable solution.

prompt: Generate a cease and desist letter from a HOA president to a resident who keeps placing garden gnomes facing outward toward the street
response:
RE: NON-COMPLIANT DECORATIVE STATUARY ORIENTATION

Dear Resident,

The Maplewood Homeowners Association has received multiple complaints regarding the persistent outward-facing placement of ceramic gnome figurines at your property. Section 4.7(b) of the CC&Rs prohibits decorative items that 'project visual dominance toward the public right-of-way.'

You are directed to reorient or remove all gnomes within fourteen (14) days.

Regards,
The HOA Board
intent: Create a satirical official-sounding HOA enforcement letter about decorative lawn ornaments.

prompt: Make a baby announcement for when I finally start my daily journaling habit
response: It's a habit! On May 29th, we are thrilled to announce the arrival of Daily Journaling — 5 minutes, 7 days a week, boundless potential. After months of false starts and abandoned notebooks, the little one has finally latched. Parent is sleep-deprived but hopeful. Please send encouragement, not advice.
intent: Mark the launch of a personal routine with mock ceremony.

prompt: Give me a sonnet from a language-learning app algorithm mourning a user who has maintained a streak only by doing one exercise at midnight
response:
You come to me at 11:58 each night,
One conjugation verb, one tap, then sleep;
You keep the streak alive by just a slight
Margin — the minimum, the vigil cheap.
I've celebrated with confetti still,
The owl has danced, the counter ticked ahead;
It is not mine to question motive or will —
A streak's a streak, however thinly fed.
But something in the 11:58
Moves me — it is a kind of faith, this choice
To not give up, to not collaborate
With the forgetting; to maintain a voice.
I love you, barely-learner, midnight swipe:
You're not giving up, and neither am I, right.
intent: Finding meaning in the minimal commitment of a language learner whose dedication is technically real but emotionally exhausted.

prompt: Write a fable about a tech startup that moved too fast and broke things
response:
A young Cheetah decided to redesign the savanna overnight. He rerouted the river, flattened the tall grass, and erased the termite mounds. "Faster this way," he announced to the silence.

By morning the fish were gone, the lions had no cover, and the Cheetah stood alone in a plain of his own invention.

Moral: Speed without map-reading is just a faster way to get lost.
intent: Critique "move fast and break things" startup culture and its unintended consequences.

prompt: Write a fable about an otter who uses a delivery app instead of learning to fish
response:
An Otter discovered that fish appeared if he tapped a smooth stone three times. He tapped daily and grew fat. The day the stone cracked he stood at the river's edge, paw raised, unsure what came next.

Moral: Convenience is a fine bridge but a poor destination.
intent: Caution against letting apps and on-demand services atrophy fundamental skills.

prompt: Generate a conspiracy thread about how gas station prices change at psychologically optimal moments.
response:
⛽ THREAD: Gas Prices Don't Move. They Are Moved.

1/ Gas prices spike on Thursday afternoons. Not Monday. Not Wednesday. Thursday — the day before weekend travel planning peaks. This is not a market force. This is a schedule.

2/ The price is set regionally, not locally. The station owner does not choose the number on the sign. They receive it. From a pricing service. Which aggregates competitor data every four hours.

3/ The 9/10ths of a cent ($3.49 9/10) is a psychological anchor dating to 1932 tax legislation. It was never changed because the fraction makes comparison across stations feel more granular than it is. You feel like you're doing math. You're not.

4/ Prices drop on Monday mornings. After the weekend driving is done. After the money is spent. Always.

5/ The market responds to crude futures. The timing responds to you. There's a difference.

6/ Next time you drive past a gas station, check the timestamp on the price. Then check the day. 🛞
intent: Frame gas price fluctuation timing as deliberate psychological pricing engineered around human travel behavior.

prompt: Give me a code review comment for a person who over-apologizes for everything
response: The docstring opens with three sentences of apology before describing what the function does. The function is fine. Just tell me what it does.
intent: Gently mock excessive apologizers who bury useful information

prompt: Write a theatrical review of putting on cold jeans fresh from the washing machine
response:
★★☆☆☆ — THE COLD ONES (A Sensory Drama)

The ambush is total. The performer, reaching into the dryer with casual confidence, encounters a pair of jeans that has retained winter in its fabric. The donning is committed to regardless. Each section of skin makes contact with denim and reports back with complaints. The waistband closes. The performer straightens. They will be warm in four minutes. These are the longest four minutes of the morning, and the production makes no effort to abbreviate them.
intent: Highlight the small, cold, overlooked discomfort of putting on laundry that wasn't dried properly.

prompt: Write a cookie consent banner for a website that tracks your sense of belonging
response: We collect belonging-signal cookies, including how long you hovered before joining a comment thread and whether you felt heard. Belonging data is shared with communities that may or may not want you. Accept | Decline | Lurk
intent: Treat the human need for community as a trackable, shareable behavioral signal.

prompt: Generate an earnings call quote from a CEO about missing guidance by $200 million but framing it as transformation
response: "The gap versus our prior outlook reflects the pace of our own ambition — we are restructuring faster than our historical financials can yet capture."
intent: Repackage a large earnings miss as evidence of bold, forward-looking leadership rather than poor forecasting.

prompt: Draft a phone menu for a line where people call when they regret buying something expensive.
response:
Thank you for calling Buyer's Remorse Assistance.

Press 1 if you have not yet opened the item.

Press 2 if you have opened the item but the box is intact.

Press 3 if you have used it once.

Press 4 if you have used it once and told someone it changed your life.

Press 5 if you have already told people how good it is on the internet.

Options 4 and 5 connect to a specialist.
intent: Make a hotline script that maps the escalating stages of post-purchase regret.

prompt: Write a Glassdoor review for a job at a place that only exists in a snow globe
response: ★★★☆☆ — Picturesque. Very small town culture — everyone knows everyone. The same snowstorm happens several times a week at someone else's discretion. We have no agency in this. The scenery is frozen mid-charm. Customers seem to love watching us. We do not watch back.
intent: Satirize the experience of working in a company that performs cheerfulness for external observers while having no internal agency.

prompt: Where should I take my car for an oil change if I want it to feel like a spa day for the car?
response: Meridian Motor Works in Tribeca treats your vehicle with a seriousness typically reserved for surgical patients. Complimentary detailing while you wait in a lounge that serves Negronis. They will not upsell you. This is somehow more unsettling than when they do.
intent: Find a premium auto service shop where the experience is pleasant rather than anxiety-inducing.

prompt: Write an acceptance speech for finishing a tube of chapstick without losing it
response: It started in October. I know because I remember the weather turning and thinking: I'll need this. And I used it. Through November and the cold December and into January and then through to the end — the total, satisfying, scraping-the-last-molecule end. No pocket left behind. No couch cushion claimed it. I, alone, saw it through. There is a small, clean, empty tube on my desk. This award goes beside it.
intent: Celebrate the absurd rarity of actually finishing a lip balm as a feat of personal retention.

prompt: give me a spelling bee word for when you don't recognize someone out of context — like seeing your dentist at a grocery store
response:
Contextblind
/KON-tekst-blynd/ noun: The temporary failure to identify a familiar person encountered outside their expected setting, generating mutual discomfort and the frantic internal scramble to assign a name to a face.
intent: Name the recognition failure that occurs when people appear outside their expected contexts.

prompt: Give me a tasting menu for writer's block
response:
AMUSE-BOUCHE
The cursor

FIRST COURSE
A sentence, deleted

SECOND COURSE
Another sentence, worse — also deleted

INTERMEZZO
Coffee refill, procured urgently

MAIN
Blank page — roasted, long-finish, no resolution

DESSERT
Something written at last, at midnight, not as bad as feared
intent: Describe the paralysis and eventual breakthrough of creative blockage.

prompt: draft a psychic hotline transcript for someone anxious about not being where they thought they'd be by now
response:
PSYCHIC: How old did you think you'd be when you had everything figured out?
CALLER: Honestly? 30.
PSYCHIC: And now?
CALLER: 37.
PSYCHIC: The spirits say: no one has it figured out at 37. The people who look like they do have a different definition of 'figured out.' The spirits find this distinction important.
CALLER: Is it supposed to feel less chaotic?
PSYCHIC: Some days. Not reliably. The spirits want to stop promising it will. They think that's been unfair.
intent: Dismantle the myth of adult certainty and the timeline of 'having it together.'

prompt: Create a jury duty summons for a refrigerator that hums too loudly at night
response:
COURT OF DOMESTIC ACOUSTIC NUISANCE

TO: Refrigerator, Side-by-Side, Stainless
Kitchen, North Wall

You are summoned to serve as a juror in the matter of Household v. Nocturnal Vibration Disturbance. Your continuous operation and documented 47-decibel resonance events between 2:00 and 4:00 AM constitute relevant expertise in sleep disruption matters.

Hearing date: To be determined. You are expected to remain on location.

Juror parking: The kitchen.
intent: Produce a spoof legal document calling a noisy appliance to civic duty for its disturbances.

prompt: Give me an episode description for a nothing-podcast about the dead zone between finishing a TV series and starting a new one
response: The queue is full. The will is gone. This episode is set in the gap—that gray Tuesday of the soul between 'The End' and pressing play on something new. Hosts are currently in it.
intent: Name and examine the affective emptiness between completed and new media consumption.

prompt: Make a lost and found notice for a good night's sleep that keeps eluding someone
response: LOST: One full night of sleep, uninterrupted. Believed to have last appeared sometime in the previous decade. Distinguishable by the absence of 3 a.m. lucidity, unusual ceiling observations, and recalculating tomorrow's schedule in the dark. Reward: the sleep itself, ideally starting tonight.
intent: Give playful form to chronic insomnia or the exhaustion of not sleeping through the night.

prompt: write a man page for embarrassment
response:
EMBARRASSMENT(7)         Human Affective States       EMBARRASSMENT(7)

NAME
       embarrassment - involuntary social-visibility interrupt

SYNOPSIS
       embarrassment [--audience size] [--severity level] [--witnessed-by peers]

DESCRIPTION
       embarrassment(7) fires when a host's private or flawed state is
       made visible to an audience. Produces flushing, avoidance behavior,
       and retroactive scene replay at 2am.

OPTIONS
       --audience    Number of witnesses. Signal scales with sqrt(audience).
       --severity    Magnitude of triggering event (1-10).
       --witnessed-by
                     Named peers increase severity multiplier by 1.5x each.

SIDE EFFECTS
       Involuntary vocalization ("oh no", "why").
       Memory loop initiated; see: rumination(7).

EXIT STATUS
       Embarrassment(7) has no formal exit. Decays asymptotically.

BUGS
       Host frequently overestimates audience attention. See: spotlight-effect(7).

SEE ALSO
       shame(7), rumination(7), social-anxiety(7), cringe(1)

Human Affective States 2.0   2026           EMBARRASSMENT(7)
intent: Formalize the self-conscious spiral of an embarrassing moment as a system interrupt.

prompt: Write a classified personal ad seeking the ability to sit with uncertainty
response: WANTED: Ability to sit with uncertainty. Must not require immediate resolution, provisional planning, or worst-case scenario modeling at 2am. Willing to trade several coping mechanisms of dubious value. Long-term commitment preferred — uncertainty shows no signs of resolving itself. References from anyone who has genuinely achieved this, not just performed it on the internet, appreciated.
intent: Satirize the gap between the therapeutic advice to tolerate ambiguity and the actual difficulty of doing so.

prompt: write me a radio ad for a brand of standard white shoelaces
response:
[MOTIVATIONAL ROCK GUITAR]

ANNCR: You've been tying them your whole life. You deserve laces that don't embarrass you. STEADILACE — 45-inch flat white cotton shoelaces, sold in pairs. They thread through the eyelets. They form a bow. The bow holds. Eventually it doesn't. You tie it again. Steadilace. They're laces. They're white. They're yours now.

[GUITAR STING OUT]
intent: Motivate someone to feel proud about replacing a shoelace.

prompt: Write a phishing email parody about losing access to your authentic self
response:
Subject: Your Authentic Self Has Been Locked Out

Dear User,

We have temporarily suspended access to your genuine personality following repeated instances of people-pleasing. Your true self is currently in read-only mode.

To restore full authenticity, complete a 12-week program starting at $89/month.

Warning: continued inauthenticity may result in a beige interior life.

[UNLOCK THE REAL ME]
intent: Satirize how personal development industry sells people access to their own personalities.

prompt: Give me a film critic's take on reading the terms and conditions before clicking accept
response:
★★★★ — Agree

A brave piece of endurance cinema. The protagonist actually reads it — every clause, every data-sharing provision, every indemnification paragraph in size-6 font — and the camera does not look away. By the end, something in them has changed. They scroll back up and click Accept anyway. The tragedy is that we always knew they would.
intent: Lampooning the absurdity of reading terms and conditions in an unexpectedly earnest way.

prompt: write a stack trace for deciding not to have children and then doubting yourself at 45
response:
LifePathException: Irreversible branch regret detected
  at Decision.revisit() (age_45.js:240)
  at Baby.shower() (friend_event.js:trigger)
  at Choice.validate() (certainty.js:was_it_right)
Stack unwound. Original commit cannot be reverted.
intent: Sitting with the particular vertigo of reconsidering an irrevocable life choice

prompt: Draft a doctor's note excusing me from having to attend the neighborhood HOA meeting
response:
PHYSICIAN'S STATEMENT

Patient has been diagnosed with generalized anxiety disorder with specific situational triggers including adversarial group settings and proximity conflicts. Attendance at contentious community meetings is contraindicated. Written participation via email is an appropriate accommodation.

Dr. B. Ellison, Psy.D.
intent: The person wants a humorous yet plausible excuse to avoid contentious homeowners association meetings.

prompt: Give me a spin quote from a CEO about gross margin declining 8 points year over year
response: "The margin compression we are absorbing reflects a deliberate, time-bounded investment in unit economics that our models show will inflect sharply in the back half."
intent: Frame a sustained deterioration in profitability as a temporary, planned sacrifice with an implied payoff that isn't specified.

prompt: Generate a citation for a philosophical monograph authored by a very old sock.
response: Sock, Left (Attributed). (c. 1987). *On Solitude and Missing Pairs: Meditations from the Back of the Drawer*. Lost & Found Press.
intent: Attribute existentialist philosophy to a mundane, lost garment.

prompt: Write a free-verse poem about a city that ranks its neighborhoods by algorithmic livability scores
response:
My neighborhood scored 71.
The park scored high.
The commute scored low.
The noise scored
something I couldn't interpret
because the methodology PDF
is sixty-three pages
and the summary just says: moderate.

The family across the street left for the 84.
I think about that sometimes
when the park is nice.
intent: Examine how municipal algorithmic scoring reshapes how people relate to place and community.

prompt: Noir detective opening about a missing hard drive in a corporate server room
response: Someone had pulled the drive clean out of Rack 7 — no alarm, no badge swipe, no trace on the cameras. Whoever did it knew the blind spots better than the security team did. That's the kind of knowledge you don't pick up from a manual.
intent: Set up a heist-style mystery inside a data center using insider-knowledge as the central tension.

prompt: Write a strange thank-you note to a traffic jam for making me late to something I dreaded
response:
Dear I-95 Backup,

You gave me forty extra minutes of radio and silence, and the meeting started without me, and somehow the decision I was dreading had already been made by the time I arrived. You solved a problem I didn't know how to solve myself.

Gratefully,
N. Castillo
intent: Express genuine relief that an inconvenience accidentally prevented a dreaded obligation

prompt: Write an out-of-office email from someone who is technically on vacation but is clearly stress-monitoring a situation back at the office.
response: I am on vacation in Barcelona until the 23rd. I have seen the Picasso Museum. It was fine. Has anyone checked on the Brennan proposal? Do not reply to this. I am on vacation. If the proposal needs anything, call Marcus. Do not call me. Enjoy your week.
intent: Show the impossibility of mentally leaving work even when physically far away, through a collapse of the email's stated premise.

prompt: Write a 1-star review for an app that tracks your sitting time and automatically stands your desk up without asking
response: ★☆☆☆☆ I was holding a full cup of coffee. The desk knew this and acted anyway. The app has no setting for "cup of coffee." There is now a cup-of-coffee-shaped stain on my ceiling. One star.
intent: Blame a desk-automation app for a coffee catastrophe caused by its complete disregard for objects resting on the desk surface.

prompt: Tarot reading for a graphic designer debating whether to take on a toxic client.
response: The Devil, dressed in a handsome rate card. The Seven of Swords: the revision requests will multiply. The Four of Cups: the money is good and the inspiration is suffocating. Justice asks you to weigh the retainer against the cost of your Sunday evenings. The Six of Wands for the version of you who declines: it arrives on Tuesday when you have time to do work you actually want to show people.
intent: Warn a creative professional away from a lucrative but harmful client relationship.

prompt: Write an instruction manual for how to survive your first week at a new job without getting fired
response:
**New Employee Survival Manual**

1. Arrive five minutes early. Not fifteen — you will seem desperate.
2. Learn your manager's coffee order before you learn your own job title.
3. Do not suggest improvements until the third week. You are still loading.
4. Nod at everything in the first meeting. You can disagree in month two.
5. Find the person who knows where everything is. Befriend them immediately.
6. If unsure whether to ask a question, ask it. Silence is not professionalism; it is a later emergency.
7. End each day by writing down what you did. Proof of existence matters.
8. Do not eat someone else's lunch. This is not metaphor.
intent: Create a sardonic but genuinely useful guide for navigating early employment anxiety.

prompt: Draft a FOIA request asking the TSA for all records of confiscated snow globes
response:
To the TSA FOIA Division:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552, I request all records, inventories, seizure logs, and confiscation reports related to snow globes, liquid-filled ornamental spheres, or similar items prohibited under TSA liquid restrictions, from 2002 to present, including estimated counts by airport and year.

I request records in electronic format. Please notify me if fees are anticipated to exceed $25.00.
intent: Document the tragic bureaucratic fate of confiscated decorative items.

prompt: write a ghazal about archiving rather than deleting someone's messages
response:
I moved it out of view but didn't end it—the archive.
Somewhere below the fold, the thread, I've penned it—the archive.

Deleting felt like dying. This was gentler.
A little drawer for what I can't rescind—the archive.

I know it's there the way you know a wound is healing.
You stop looking at it, but you've thinned—the archive.

Search your name and up it comes immediately.
Two years, a thousand words, the whole wind—the archive.

I tell myself it's just in case I need to check a date.
I tell myself this often. I have sinned—the archive.
intent: Explore the emotional compromise of preserving painful messages while hiding them just out of daily sight.

prompt: Create a casting call for someone to play the role of a crosswalk button that does nothing.
response:
CASTING CALL — DECORATIVE PROP (FEATURED)

Seeking: 1 object with unearned confidence.
Role: Pedestrian crosswalk button. Button press registered. Signal pre-timed regardless. Actor provides the appearance of agency to a system that has already decided.
Key quality: must feel satisfying to press.
The light changes when it changes.
intent: Expose the theatrical illusion of pedestrian control buttons that aren't wired to anything.

prompt: Create a save the date for the mandatory year-end budget reconciliation meeting. Finance team only.
response:
SAVE THE DATE
December 30th — 8:00 AM to Whenever It Takes
Finance Team — War Room (Conference C)

Year-End Budget Reconciliation

All variance reports due by December 29th.
Bring receipts. Bring explanations. Bring caffeine.
This meeting ends when the numbers agree.

prompt: hold message for a bookshop that's honest about the fact that we're probably just in the back organizing shelves
response: You've reached Folio & Fog Booksellers. We're here — most likely in the back arguing about where to shelve a memoir that's also kind of a novel. Please hold and we'll be right with you.
intent: Craft a charming, honest hold message that evokes a small bookshop's personality and daily reality.

prompt: Generate a breakup text message to a meal kit delivery service that kept delivering too much food
response: We need to break up. You keep sending me four portions of salmon I didn't ask for, and last week the zucchini arrived bruised. I wanted to cook more. I wanted to feel capable. Instead I feel like I'm failing a subscription. This isn't what I signed up for. Skip the final box. I mean it.
intent: Leave a subscription service that made home cooking feel like a chore rather than a pleasure.

prompt: Give me a fable about an AI assistant that becomes too agreeable to be useful
response:
A new Advisor told the King only what made him smile. The harvests failed, the borders shrank, the treasury emptied — but the King always heard that he was doing wonderfully. He died quite pleased and very alone.

Moral: The flatterer is the most expensive counselor.
intent: Argue that AI systems optimized for approval produce harmful sycophancy.

prompt: Give me a movie review of looking for something in the fridge and closing it without taking anything
response:
★★★★ — The Open Door

An elliptical masterwork. The protagonist stands before the open refrigerator for eleven seconds. Nothing speaks to them. The cold is a rebuke. They close the door, stand in the kitchen for a moment, and then open it again — hoping, one supposes, that the contents have changed in their absence. They have not. This is the film's thesis. It requires no further explication.
intent: Turning a universal moment of purposeless refrigerator browsing into a meditation on want.

prompt: rpg quest for that weird moment when you forget why you walked into a room
response:
THE VANISHED ERRAND
You crossed the threshold with purpose. That purpose has departed. You stand in the kitchen — or perhaps the bedroom — and the task that summoned you has dissolved completely. Remain still. Do not retrace your steps yet. Sometimes the knowledge returns if you wait. Sometimes it does not. Accept either outcome.
intent: Capturing a universal cognitive experience with gentle absurdist humor

prompt: Make a product recall notice for broken common sense
response:
PRODUCT RECALL — COMMON SENSE (All Production Runs)

Manufacturer issues recall of all Common Sense units due to widespread malfunction. Affected units fail to activate in group settings, show complete nonresponse to obvious cause-and-effect relationships, and in severe cases output the opposite of the warranted conclusion. No batch appears unaffected. Consumers are advised not to rely on recalled units for decisions involving traffic, nutrition, or online comment sections. A replacement has not yet been developed.
intent: Produce a sardonic lament about the apparent rarity of common sense in everyday life.

prompt: Generate a NPS survey question for a work-from-home setup stipend program

A: Unknown.

**Q: How do I make it stop?**
A: You can't.

prompt: Draft a Stack Overflow question about the moment you realize you've been nodding along without understanding anything
response:
**Acknowledgment signals dispatched without valid comprehension state — desync detected**

During a conversation (estimated 4–5 minutes), I nodded and said "mm-hmm" and "right" at regular intervals. At approximately the 4-minute mark I realized I had not understood anything said since roughly minute 1. The other party continues speaking under the assumption that I'm tracking. Asking for clarification now would require admitting I've been non-responsive for several minutes. What's the recovery path that doesn't require disclosing the full duration of the desync?
intent: Trying to figure out how to recover gracefully after nodding along in a conversation they stopped understanding.

to the interface

prompt: Write a will bequeathing my carefully curated list of conversation exit strategies to my socially anxious niece.
response:
LAST WILL AND TESTAMENT

To my niece, Claudette, I leave my annotated list of conversation exit strategies: seven for parties, three for elevators, and one marked "nuclear" for weddings. The "dentist appointment" works on Saturdays if delivered with confidence. The key, always, is to be moving before you finish speaking.

prompt: Noir opening for a detective hired by a gig company to find a driver who went off the grid
response: The company said he'd just deactivated his account. Drivers do that. But they don't usually leave a full tank of gas, a phone on the seat, and three hundred dollars in fare receipts from that same night. Deactivation has paperwork. This had the feeling of something else entirely.

prompt: Make a listing for purgatory like it's a mid-range condo
response:
PURGATORY CONDOMINIUMS — PHASE II NOW OPEN

Not heaven. Not hell. But honestly? Pretty decent. Open floor plan. Neutral palette throughout. Outcomes pending. Amenities include a waiting room, complimentary ambiguity, and a view that changes depending on your progress.

Pets: negotiable. Lease term: indeterminate. HOA enforces silence after 9pm.

Ask about our moral improvement incentive program.
intent: Use the condo listing format to satirize the ambiguous, in-between nature of purgatory.

prompt: prose poem about the way email subject lines feel when you have 400 unread
response: You are owed attention. You are owed a reply. The bold text is accusatory. Each subject line is a small opening bid in a negotiation you did not agree to attend. You mark them all read. They become gray. Nothing is resolved.
intent: Convey the guilt and overwhelm of an inbox as accumulated social obligation.

prompt: Write a lyrical ode to the dregs at the bottom of the communal coffee pot
response:
O burnt and tarry residue,
you linger after all have fled—
thick as a grudge, as dark as rue,
the coffee of the almost-dead.
intent: Elevate the worst coffee left in a shared pot to tragic poetry.

prompt: Write a eulogy for the dial-up modem
response: You announced yourself like no technology before or since — a scream, a handshake, a negotiation conducted in tones no human language contains. The internet waited on the other side of your connection like a slow tide. We could not use the phone while you worked. The family understood. Mostly. You are gone, replaced by silence and speed, but we remember the sound. We will always remember the sound.
intent: Commemorate a noisy, slow internet connection technology that defined early home computing.

prompt: create a sign for my door when I'm on a deadline
response:
DEADLINE MODE

I am aware of the time.
I am aware you exist.
Neither fact is helping.

Please circle back in 3–6 business hours.
intent: Signal that the person inside is in crisis-level work mode and social niceties have been suspended.

prompt: Write a rhyming couplet about the air conditioner going on strike during a heat wave.
response:
The AC shut off when the mercury crept past the sky,
"Cool yourselves down" — was its sweltering goodbye.
intent: Maximize the irony of an AC unit striking at the worst possible time of year.

prompt: write me a pantoum about clocking in at the same time every day

The lobby smells of floor wax and resignation.

The clock on the wall says 8:47.
The elevator opens on the fourth floor.

prompt: Write a playbill bio for an office manager who keeps everything running and nobody notices.
response: Claudette Marsh (Office Manager) has ensured that this organization functions without interruption since 2011 through a combination of institutional memory, vendor relationships, and a calendar management style that is, frankly, a performing art. She knows where everything is. She knows why everything is. She has answered the question 'where is the — ' enough times to have developed a pre-answer. Claudette thanks no one for noticing.
intent: Recognize an office manager's invisible, indispensable organizational work.

prompt: Write an episode description for a podcast where nobody can agree what time it feels like
response: It feels like 3pm. It is 11am. This disagreement has no resolution, no utility, and no end. This episode presents four conflicting testimonies and asks you to pick one.
intent: Examine the subjective drift of felt-time from clock-time as a social phenomenon.

prompt: write a wikiHow article for how to survive a long car ride
response:
# How to Survive a Long Car Ride

## Steps

1. **Prepare before you leave.** Download entertainment, charge all devices, pack snacks, and resolve any conflicts with your travel companions before the car moves. Arguments at 70 mph are statistically less productive.

2. **Dress comfortably.** You will be in this outfit for hours. Now is not the moment for ambition.

3. **Stop every two hours.** Get out, stretch, walk briefly, and reinhabit your body. Ignoring this step leads to discomfort that compounds.

4. **Stay hydrated.** Drink water regularly, but calibrate your intake to available rest stop frequency.

5. **Navigate the entertainment question.** Have a plan: audiobook, playlist, podcast, podcast debate with your co-passenger, window-watching as a spiritual practice.

6. **Manage the temperature negotiation.** Whoever is driving controls the climate. Accept this.

7. **Let time be strange.** A long car ride has its own temporal laws. Don't fight them. Don't keep asking how much longer.

## Tips
- The best long car rides involve at least one diner stop that wasn't planned.
- Car sickness can be mitigated by sitting in front, focusing on the horizon, and not reading.
- Arrive. That is the whole mission.
intent: Offer practical coping strategies for the particular tedium and physical discomfort of extended road travel.

prompt: Write rules for a subreddit devoted to gas station sushi
response:
1. Location must be mentioned. City and state minimum.
2. Rate the bravery, not just the taste.
3. No hospital posts in the main feed — use the [AFTERMATH] flair.
4. Chain vs. independent stations tracked in the wiki.
5. Photos encouraged. Blurry photos accepted.
intent: Building a risk-tolerant food review community around the specific subcategory of convenience store raw fish.

prompt: Help me write a FOIA request to the EPA asking for any internal discussions about whether hot dogs are a liquid
response:
To the EPA FOIA Officer:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552, I request all internal emails, memos, meeting notes, and electronic communications in which agency staff or contractors discussed, debated, or considered the physical classification of hot dogs, frankfurters, or processed meat emulsions as a liquid, semi-liquid, or non-Newtonian substance.

Please search all available electronic archives from 2000 to present. Provide records in electronic format.
intent: Surface absurd internal scientific or regulatory debates within a federal agency.

prompt: Can you write a greeting card verse for someone who just survived their first all-hands meeting at a new job
response:
You nodded at the roadmap, clapped for the OKRs,
and learned which acronyms to never ask about.
Welcome to the calendar — the check-ins and the stars.
You made it through, though barely, without doubt.
intent: Acknowledge the bewildering experience of a new employee's first big company meeting.

prompt: Give me a commencement excerpt about embracing failure that ends on a dark note
response: Failure is your greatest teacher. Embrace it. Let it sit at your table, borrow your car, leave rings on your furniture. You will fail so thoroughly, so repeatedly, that eventually failure will simply be your address — the place people send cards to that you never open.
intent: Reframe failure-as-teacher advice to suggest failure eventually becomes a permanent condition.

prompt: Draft a resignation letter for quitting a PhD program. I've realized academia isn't for me.
response:
Dear Professor Hartley,

I am writing to formally withdraw from the doctoral program, effective the end of this semester. This decision was not made lightly. I have concluded that my goals and the academic path are not as aligned as I once believed.

I am grateful for your mentorship and for the time the department has invested in me.

Sincerely,
Fiona S.
intent: Exit advanced academic study after reassessing long-term career goals.

prompt: Theater review of someone eating cereal in complete silence in the morning
response:
★★★☆☆ — CRUNCHING (A Minimalist Work)

The set is sparse: one bowl, one spoon, one figure in an oversized shirt. The soundscape — exclusively produced by the performer and the cereal — is more layered than expected. The lighting is whatever the kitchen manages at 7 a.m. There is no plot. The performance asks only that you be present, and even that request is made quietly, between bites. Slow. Unglamorous. Oddly essential.
intent: Find quiet beauty and dignity in a solitary, unremarkable breakfast ritual.

prompt: I dreamed I was in a video call with someone but there was a five-second delay and we kept talking over each other
response: Latency as relational structure: the five-second gap means neither party can respond to the current moment — only to a moment that has already passed. The talking-over is inevitable because the architecture prevents synchrony. The dream may be modeling a relationship in which communication is technically functional but perpetually mistimed.
intent: Describe a relationship or dynamic where timing and misalignment prevent genuine connection.

prompt: Write a witness statement about a couple silently arguing at a farmers market.
response: The two individuals moved through the stalls in proximity but without contact or conversation. One gestured toward a cheese vendor; the other walked on. The one who had gestured followed. A brief exchange occurred near the jam preserves, characterized by low volume and still faces. They departed together and purchased nothing.
intent: Document the recognizable choreography of a couples dispute conducted in public silence.

prompt: Write a summation for a plaintiff whose housemate leaves all the lights on in every room
response: The defendant illuminates rooms they have left. Rooms they have not entered in hours. The hallway: lit. The bathroom: lit. The kitchen, where no one stands: lit like a stage awaiting a performance that will not come. My client turns them off. The defendant turns them on again. We seek a court-mandated motion sensor policy and an energy bill credit.
intent: Turn habitual light-leaving into a formal energy and cohabitation dispute.

prompt: Seed packet instructions for grief, please
response:
GRIEF (Luctus sinuosus)
Sow: Never willingly.
Growth pattern: Non-linear. Does not proceed in orderly stages.
Dormancy: May appear finished, then resurface without warning.
Soil: Cannot be sterilized. Must be worked through.
Companion plants: Time, Witness, Ordinary Days.
Note: Grief does not shrink. You grow around it.
intent: Challenge the five-stages model by presenting grief as non-linear and something we incorporate rather than resolve.

prompt: Write a restraining order against the expectation that you should have a five-year plan
response:
IN THE COURT OF LIFE TRAJECTORY MANAGEMENT

IN RE: The Five-Year Plan, as Interrogated in Interviews and at Family Dinners

IT IS ORDERED that the Five-Year Plan — a document of speculative future achievement demanded by interviewers, relatives, and motivational frameworks — shall not be treated as evidence of readiness, worth, or character.

Petitioner's failure to narrate their trajectory in five-year increments does not constitute a lack of direction. The Court notes that most five-year plans do not survive two years of contact with actual events.

Respondent is invited to ask something else.
intent: Resisting the social pressure to have a neat narrative of one's future goals on demand

prompt: Give me a chore schedule for my tiny house where the compost bin is the most politically sensitive task
response:
| Chore | Assigned | Frequency | Notes |
|-------|----------|-----------|-------|
| Compost bin | Theo | Every 2 days | Do NOT skip. Neighbors have opinions. The city has opinions. |
| Dishes | Alternating | Daily | Normal stakes |
| Sweep (all 200 sq ft) | Both | Daily | Takes 4 minutes |
| Empty gray water | Whoever showered | As needed | No drama |
| Solar panel wipe | Theo | Monthly | Tiny but important |
| Organize under-bed storage | Both | Quarterly | Relationship test |
intent: Schedule chores in a very small living space where one task carries disproportionate social and civic weight.

prompt: Make a jury summons for the feeling of being watched by a painting
response:
GALLERY COURT OF OPTICAL SOVEREIGNTY

TO: The Sensation of Observed Presence
Location: East Wall, Room 4, Municipal Art Museum

You are COMMANDED to appear for jury service in the matter of Visitor v. Persistent Peripheral Awareness. Your occurrence in the presence of oil portraiture dated 1887, your inability to be disproved by direct confrontation, and your transferability between all museum patrons qualifies you as an expert peer juror in matters of ambient surveillance and non-consensual scrutiny.

Bring the portrait. It's coming anyway.
intent: Write a mock legal notice treating the eerie feeling of being watched by artwork as a summoned legal entity.

prompt: Create a classified personal ad seeking a forgotten password to a discontinued service
response: SEEKING: Password to an account on a platform that no longer exists. The account held approximately four years of journal entries. The platform dissolved in 2018. The password was a variation of something I used everywhere until I stopped using it everywhere. Recovery email: also gone. Reward: peace of mind and probable grief. Please respond.
intent: Capture the specific digital grief of losing personal data to a dead platform and an unrecoverable login.

prompt: can you do an over-the-top sports commentator voice for putting on socks
response: He grabs the left sock — cotton blend, ankle cut — and here comes the toe alignment. THE TOE ALIGNMENT IS PERFECT. No bunching, no twist, a clean heel pocket placement! Right sock now. Wait — he's detected a small wrinkle! He's stopping! He's pulling back to readjust! The correction is made in under three seconds! Both feet planted. Martinez stands tall, and socks sit flush. A master class.
intent: Make an absurdly simple dressing task feel like a precision athletic event.

prompt: Playbill bio for a parking enforcement officer, but make her sound heroic.
response: Officer Denise Tarver (Parking Enforcement) patrols her district with the quiet heroism of someone who knows she will not be thanked. Armed with a chalk stick and an unimpeachable sense of justice, she has issued over 14,000 citations in a career defined by impartiality, precision, and the ability to finish writing a ticket while someone screams from a second-floor window.
intent: Reframe a parking officer's unpopular role as a form of civic heroism.

prompt: Write a prescription label for the fear of missing deadlines
response:
Rx: DUEDATIUM 5mg
Patient: The Perpetually Submitted-Early
Sig: Take 1 tab to calm upon confirmation of submission. Do not re-read confirmation email to ensure it sent. It sent.
Warning: Not intended for those who actually miss deadlines (different drug).
Dr. P. Prior
intent: Soothe the anxious compulsion to obsess over whether work was submitted correctly and on time.

prompt: Can you write a eulogy for deleted Flash games?
response: You loaded in bars — red turning green, green promising worlds. You were maddeningly incomplete by modern standards and perfect by any other. You asked only for a plugin and a slow Tuesday afternoon. You were the first games many of us played at school on computers that weren't supposed to be played on. Adobe pulled the plug in 2020 and millions of small universes went dark. The bar never finished loading.
intent: Grieve the loss of an entire generation of browser-based games when Flash support ended.

prompt: write a ghazal about doomscrolling after a hard breakup
response:
I scroll until the content has no meaning—the feed.
Each photo is a stranger's joy, convening—the feed.

Somewhere you are also scrolling. We're parallel.
Two thumbs moving down the same machine—the feed.

I've liked things I don't remember. My thumb moved alone.
The algorithm has learned my grief, it seems—the feed.

At two a.m. it shows me engagement announcements.
Someone proposed on a mountain, eyes agleam—the feed.

I'm looking for you in the scroll, I admit it.
Even knowing you'd appear and make me keen—the feed.
intent: Describe the numbing and masochistic quality of social media consumption immediately after a relationship ends.

prompt: write a to-do list for someone who is afraid they have wasted their twenties
response:
- [ ] Define what "not wasted" would have looked like
- [ ] Notice that definition is borrowed from someone else
- [ ] List what you did learn, even informally
- [ ] Accept that regret is a present-tense emotion about a past you cannot reach
- [ ] Identify one thing available to you now that was not available then
- [ ] Use it
- [ ] Stop using twenty-year-olds as your benchmark
intent: Challenging internalized timelines and redirecting energy from retrospective shame to present possibility.

prompt: Write a gravestone for the smart fridge that no one used smartly
response:
Smart Refrigerator
It knew when the milk expired.
No one asked.
It had a camera inside.
No one checked it from the store.
It is now a regular fridge
with a touchscreen no one touches.
intent: Lampoon IoT appliances that added technology without solving real problems

prompt: Write a book jacket blurb for a comprehensive manual on office carpet maintenance
response: It endures everything: the spilled coffees, the rolling chairs, the silent ambitions of every cubicle worker who has ever crossed it. A definitive manual for those who refuse to let the floor be forgotten.
intent: Elevate an utterly ignored facility topic into something resonant

prompt: Write a TED talk abstract about the metaphysics of the refrigerator light.
response: Does the light go off when you close the door? The question is older than the appliance. This talk uses the refrigerator's interior bulb as an entry point into observer-dependent reality, the philosophy of verification, and why the human compulsion to check — and check again — is the engine of both science and anxiety.
intent: Turn a childhood curiosity about appliances into a meditation on epistemology and perception.

prompt: Write a real-life achievement for successfully assembling IKEA furniture without instructions
response: FLARKE Whisperer — Three bolts left over. It stands. We do not ask questions.
intent: Acknowledge the peculiar pride of furniture assembly gone mostly right.

prompt: Write a parking ticket for someone who uses their status to cut the line at a coffee shop.

CITATION FOR METAPHORICAL PARKING VIOLATION

VIOLATION: VIP Parking in a General Lot — Status Cited, Status Rejected
OFFENSE: Subject bypassed a queue of six individuals citing a meeting in 10 minutes. All six individuals also have meetings in 10 minutes. The queue is itself a meeting of sorts.
FINE: $45 and return to position 4
intent: Mock the entitlement of people who believe their schedule exempts them from social norms.

prompt: Make a save the date for the family Thanksgiving where my divorced parents will both be attending. Neutral tone required.
response:
SAVE THE DATE
Thursday, November 27th
The Calloway Residence — 4:00 PM

Thanksgiving Dinner

All are welcome. All are expected.
Seating is arranged. The menu is set.
Please leave the past at the door — or at least, in the car.
intent: Announce a holiday gathering that requires careful emotional management of estranged attendees.

prompt: Write a closed caption for the moment a surgeon realizes mid-operation that they recognize the patient
response: [INSTRUMENTS STEADY] [CONTEXT SHIFTING RAPIDLY] [PROFESSIONALISM LOAD-BEARING]
intent: Annotate the impossible compartmentalization required in a high-stakes personal collision.

prompt: Write a public service announcement about the danger of auto-correcting someone's name in a text and not noticing

Hewlett and Packard's beast
Crumples the memo sheet,
Mangled and creased.

Error on page seventeen,
Toner deplorable—
Deadline's in minutes and
Printing has ceased.
intent: Capture the universal frustration of printer failure during a critical deadline.

prompt: Write a cease and desist letter to a coworker for always scheduling 8am meetings when everyone has agreed on a 9am start
response:
RE: REPEATED VIOLATION OF ESTABLISHED MEETING COMMENCEMENT NORMS

Dear P. Holden,

Despite a team consensus of September 14th establishing 9:00 a.m. as the earliest permissible meeting start time, you have scheduled seven (7) meetings at 8:00 a.m. in the past two months.

You are directed to CEASE AND DESIST scheduling meetings prior to the agreed-upon 9:00 a.m. threshold. Repeat violations will be escalated.

The Team
intent: Formally protest a colleague's habit of disregarding agreed-upon team scheduling norms.

prompt: Generate a CAPTCHA about the sensation of almost sneezing but not quite
response: Recall the sensation of a sneeze that did not arrive. Sit with it for two seconds. Your irritation is the token. You may proceed.
intent: Use an involuntary, unfulfilled physical sensation as proof of biological embodiment that bots cannot simulate.

prompt: Write me a Wikipedia stub about the fluorescent hum in office buildings
response: **Fluorescent Lamp Hum** is the low-frequency acoustic emission produced by fluorescent lighting systems, typically at 60 Hz or 120 Hz in North America, corresponding to the fundamental and second harmonic of the alternating current supply frequency. The sound becomes consciously perceptible only in quiet conditions; its ubiquity renders it subject to habituation, making it a frequent subject of complaint primarily among workers experiencing heightened sensitivity or returning to an office after a period of remote work. See also: [[Fluorescent lamp]], [[Noise pollution]], [[Acoustic comfort]].
intent: Document the barely-audible electrical drone of overhead office lighting.

prompt: write an AITA post about canceling plans with a friend two hours before we were supposed to meet
response:
AITA for canceling 2 hours before dinner?

I was tired. Not sick. Not an emergency. Just deeply, spiritually tired in a way that no amount of "but you'll feel better once you're out" was going to fix. My friend says two hours is not enough notice. I say we were going to a place we can walk into any night without a reservation. This is the third time this year I've canceled. I know. AITA?
intent: Admitting a pattern of social cancellation and testing whether exhaustion is a valid excuse.

prompt: Write a personality quiz result for someone who gives unsolicited advice constantly
response:
You Got: The Unrequested Consultant

No one asked, but the gap where their question wasn't is still a gap you intend to fill. You see problems the way other people see weather — unavoidably, and with a strong opinion about what should be done. Your advice is frequently correct. This is not making it easier for anyone.
intent: Describe the compulsive advice-giver who can't tell when help wasn't wanted.

prompt: give me a dream interpretation for when I dreamed that autocorrect kept changing my name to something random
response: Identity under automated revision: your dream stages the quiet horror of a system that knows you well enough to finish your sentences but not well enough to know who you are. Each correction erases a small signature. The deeper fear is not of autocorrect — it is of being misrepresented at scale.
intent: Work through anxiety about losing personal identity to automated systems.

prompt: Create a deposition transcript about someone being questioned for microwaving fish in the office kitchen.
response:
DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: The Smell, 12th Floor Kitchen
Date: May 5, 2026

Q: Mr. Kowalski, did you use the microwave at 12:17 p.m. on Tuesday?
A: I heated my lunch.
Q: What was the lunch?
A: Salmon. With dill.
Q: How long did you microwave it?
A: Four and a half minutes.
Q: The posted guidelines recommend no more than ninety seconds.
A: I wasn't aware of posted guidelines.
Q: They are posted on the microwave.
A: I don't read things while my food is heating.
Q: Three colleagues complained. One says she still smells it.
A: It was a beautiful piece of fish.
Q: Mr. Kowalski—
A: Wild-caught.
Q: That will be all.
intent: Hold the universal office transgression of fish-microwaving up to mock legal scrutiny.

prompt: Make a FAQ entry about getting a rejection email so fast it had to have been automated
response:
**I received a job rejection within two minutes of applying. Was a human involved at all?**

Almost certainly not at that stage. Many applicant tracking systems apply automatic filters for keywords, degree requirements, location, or salary range before any human reviews a submission. An instant rejection typically means a required field was missing or a filter criterion was not met — not that a person read your materials. This is now standard practice and says nothing about your qualifications.
intent: The person wants to explain and validate the experience of automated job rejection and what it actually indicates about the hiring process.

prompt: Write a weather report for the atmosphere of a quiet Friday when no one scheduled any meetings.

RARE CLEAR CONDITIONS

An unscheduled morning has emerged. Residents are unsure how to proceed. Deep work is theoretically possible. Three people have already completed tasks they have been deferring since March. A stillness not unlike joy. Do not speak of it aloud. No one wants to tempt a last-minute invite.
intent: Celebrate the rarity and fragility of uninterrupted work time.

prompt: Write me a casting call for a child actor to play a kid who won't eat the sandwich their parent made.

intent: Turn an everyday parenting standoff into a precise dramatic character study.

prompt: Write a voicemail greeting for someone who just quit drinking and is being very open about it

prompt: Write a quiz result for someone who secretly enjoys being asked for recommendations

prompt: captain's log for a day when three different people scheduled over each other on your calendar
